chaptername: Preface: The Appearing of White Tara
chapterbody: n the past, when I was at my teacher, Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye's place, he told me a true story.
chapterbody 3: Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye said that one day White Tara appeared to him and told him: "You must quickly go to Taiwan, to find someone with the family name of Luo. In this present age, this Mr. Luo will be a rare and precious acharya, and in the future he will attain accomplishment. This acharya's bliss-body is none other than Amitabha Buddha."
chapterbody 5: At the beginning, Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye did not take this seriously. However, White Tara appeared to him repeatedly. Hence my teacher brought his attendant Thubten Qi-gong with him to Taiwan to disseminate Buddhadharma, while looking for someone with the last name of "Luo" at the same time. He searched day after day. But he could not find someone with the last name of "Luo." Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye was very upset that he could not find someone with the last name of "Luo."
chapterbody 7: Not long after, I took refuge under Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye of my own accord. My teacher gave me the dharma name, "Thubten Qi-mo."
chapterbody 9: One day, White Tara showed up again. My teacher told White Tara: "While in Taiwan, I could not find someone with the family name of 'Luo.'"
chapterbody 10: White Tara replied: "He is already by your side, and is none other than Sheng-Yen Lu. Go on and teach him the profound esoteric keys of the three inner secret dharma practices."
chapterbody 11: My teacher said: "His family name is Lu, not Luo?" White Tara replied: "In Taiwanese, the pronunciation of the family name Lu is Luo." "Lu" is pronounced as "Luo" in Taiwanese.
chapterbody 12: Suddenly, light dawned upon Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye.
chapterbody 13: As a result, I became his main disciple at his place, and received the pivotal keys of the inner secret practices all through his teachings, incomparable empowerments and continuous blessings.
chapterbody 15: At Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye's place, I received:
chapterbody 17: Kurukulle Buddha Mother empowerment
chapterbody 18: The Highest Yoga Tantra empowerment
chapterbody 19: Yamantaka empowerment
chapterbody 20: The great Kalachakra empower ...
chapterbody 22: Even after the parinirvana of Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye, he still appeared to teach me uncommon dharma teachings. I carry him above my crown in gratitude.
chapterbody 26: One day, White Tara appeared and spoke to me:
chapterbody 27: "Grandmaster Lu, you have to write a book. You have the obligation to write such a book."
chapterbody 29: "What book?"
chapterbody 31: From the emptiness above, White Tara tossed a book down to me. The moment I flipped open the book, I was greatly appalled!
chapterbody 33: White Tara said:
chapterbody 34: "Write it! This is a warning to your holy disciples, who are in dangerous situations or have gone astray. This is a way to call them back onto the right path!"
chapterbody 36: I said:
chapterbody 37: "This! This is too awkward!"
chapterbody 39: White Tara said:
chapterbody 40: "Doing this awkward task is also an act of kindness! This book is one that people need to read."
chapterbody 42: Contact information for Living Buddha Lian Sheng:
chapterbody 43: Sheng-Yen Lu
chapterbody 44: 17102 NE 40th Court
chapterbody 45: Redmond, WA 98052
chapterbody 46: U.S.A.

chaptername: 001 The Book Tossed Down by White Tara
chapterbody: s mentioned in the preface, White Tara tossed a book down to me from the emptiness. As I flipped through it, I was shocked.
chapterbody 4: As I paged through the book, I was overwhelmed by horror, completely at a loss.
chapterbody 6: Many Buddhist practitioners, from taking refuge in the Vajra Master to taking refuge in Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, from cultivating external practices to internal practices, to secret practices, to the most secret practices, maintain unshakable faith in the Three Jewels.
chapterbody 7: They pray and make offerings to their teacher and the Three Jewels. These are all a necessary part of cultivation. However, they forget that "abiding by precepts" is the foundation of cultivation.
chapterbody 8: Because they did not "abide by the precepts," thus in terms of "karmic affinity," they will fall into the three lower realms.
chapterbody 9: These three lower realms are the realms of:
chapterbody 10: Hell
chapterbody 12: Animals
chapterbody 13: Listed in the book were some examples of "causes" and "consequences." What struck my eyes and appalled me, was the sight of a number of listed names that were not just familiar, but very familiar. Because of this, tears streamed down my face!
chapterbody 14: How could they be saved? What could be done? These disciples that I knew well had "fallen into the three lower realms," and had fallen with certainty. How could they be saved? The answer was:
chapterbody 19: What is "Re-Purification?"
chapterbody 20: It is remorse for the precepts one has violated, and prompt repentance before the buddhas and bodhisattvas.
chapterbody 21: The asking of forgiveness for one's transgressions from one's Root Guru in person; followed by renewed refuge and empowerment, and the vow to keep the five precepts, eight precepts, and bodhisattva vows, to practice the ten virtuous deeds, and to engender bodhicitta. This must continue until one receives a sign of acceptance for one's repentance.
chapterbody 22: When this occurs, one has now avoided the dangerous ground and wayward paths that lead down into the three lower realms.
chapterbody 23: Otherwise:
chapterbody 24: One would definitely fall into the three lower realms, without any chance of reprieve.
chapterbody 28: As Buddhists, we understand that:
chapterbody 29: Keeping the five precepts - human realm
chapterbody 30: Practicing the ten virtuous deeds - heavenly realm Following the
chapterbody 31: Theravada precepts - arhat
chapterbody 32: Cultivating the twelve links of dependent arising - pratyekabuddha
chapterbody 33: Practicing the six paramitas - bodhisattva
chapterbody 34: Engendering great bodhicitta - buddha
chapterbody 36: Many numerous buddhas and bodhisattvas manifest in a variety of physical appearances and forms, to teach us to guard and keep different types of vows and precepts. Our understanding of Buddha's teachings should be "in depth," but cultivation begins from following the "precepts."
chapterbody 37: (In other words, this means to start cultivating from the most basic precepts)
chapterbody 38: When Shakyamuni Buddha was about to enter nirvana, he left behind a most important message, "to rely upon the precepts as your teacher." This was the Buddha's instruction.
chapterbody 39: The Buddha with unsurpassed and perfect enlightenment, left behind only one word - "precepts." How can I and other Buddhists not follow it?
chapterbody 40: If one does not abide by precepts, and is able to accomplish buddhahood, neither I nor others will believe it.
chapterbody 42: The book indicates clearly:
chapterbody 43: Cultivate the practice of abiding by the precepts, [with awareness of] what one should avoid, and what one should comply with. Only by practicing in such a manner, will one be able to remain on the path of right dharma.
chapterbody 44: The power of abiding by precepts is boundless. The relinquishment of inappropriate and negative conduct is in fact a dharma remedy for worries and troubles. May everyone do one's best to abide by the precepts together!
chapterbody 45: Abiding by precepts is noble.
chapterbody 46: Abiding by precepts is purity.
chapterbody 47: Abiding by precepts is merit.
chapterbody 48: Only by cultivating in this manner, will one be able to advance in one's practice.
chapterbody 51: Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms.

chapterbody: n the book , several notable people were listed, whose names I will not disclose.
chapterbody 2: One was a "treasurer," who was in debt and consequently embezzled the trust fund and fled. My goodness! The money he misappropriated belonged to a cultivation venue!
chapterbody 3: One was a "chapter chairperson," who changed the shared ownership of the cultivation venue to a private ownership, which meant that the entire cultivation venue was now taken over as personal property.
chapterbody 4: One was a "dharma propagator," who devoured an entire temple, and ran away from under the banner of True Buddha School.
chapterbody 6: All of them are listed in the .
chapterbody 7: Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms
chapterbody 8: In the past, I have mentioned in my dharma talks:
chapterbody 9: "Public security is comparatively better in Japan, there are relatively fewer robbers and thieves. Why is that? It is because the Japanese are educated from a young age not to take things that do not belong to them!"
chapterbody 10: It is that apparent.
chapterbody 12: It is that pure.
chapterbody 13: However -
chapterbody 14: Why is it that some people can never learn?
chapterbody 18: I have once said this to people:
chapterbody 19: "One is Buddhadharma that liberates one from life and death, another is a gilded shining temple. Which of these two would you choose?"
chapterbody 20: Those with wisdom, would choose "release from life and death."
chapterbody 21: Those with malfunctioned heads, would choose "temple property."
chapterbody 22: I would say thus:
chapterbody 23: The key points of Buddhism are:
chapterbody 24: A transcendental mind of renunciation.
chapterbody 25: A mind of bodhicitta.
chapterbody 26: Right views and the Middle Path.
chapterbody 27: If cultivators in this world quarrel endlessly over temple property or even purloin it, isn't this a very shameful event? It is really absurd.
chapterbody 28: Temples are worldly objects.
chapterbody 29: Temples stand upon the great earth.
chapterbody 30: Temples cannot be brought away by anyone after death.
chapterbody 31: Temples, chapters, associations, money and property are all like this.
chapterbody 33: Therefore, in Grandmaster Lu's heart, there is only Buddhadharma; the heart dwells upon the extraordinary unsurpassed enlightenment. Other than this, what else is there?
chapterbody 34: All sentient beings, especially cultivators, should know not to be attached to "secular matters," "material possessions," "money and property."
chapterbody 35: If one does not cultivate a transcendental mind of renunciation, and instead craves for the worldly and secular, then one has broken the precepts! White Tara mentions in the book:
chapterbody 36: "In particular, for the theft of public resources and property intended for cultivation, such karmic transgressions will be far worse."
chapterbody 37: In hell:
chapterbody 38: That "treasurer" would be made to swallow molten copper, scorching his throat.
chapterbody 39: That "chapter chairperson" would be immersed in a pot of boiling oil.
chapterbody 40: That "dharma propagator" would have to undergo the daily torture of a flywheel cutting his brain open, due to the malfunction of his mind.
chapterbody 44: Have you not heard this verse?
chapterbody 45: One grain of rice from a donor,
chapterbody 46: Is as immense as Mount Meru.
chapterbody 47: If one does not cultivate,
chapterbody 48: One will have to pay through a body of fur and horns.
chapterbody 50: If a single grain of rice carries such weight, how would you possibly be able to pay back the money and property belonging to a cultivation venue, a whole chapter, or an entire temple?
chapterbody 51: Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms
chapterbody 52: Please ask yourself honestly, in the middle of the night. The things that you enjoy in the secular world, the things you use, eat, and sleep upon - are these what you deserve?

chaptername: 003 Breaking Vows and Precepts Pertaining to One's Root Guru
chapterbody 2: "With regard to Grandmaster Lu, an emanation body of Amitabha Buddha, once one has taken refuge, one can only guard and protect one's vows and precepts to the Root Guru."
chapterbody 3: Why is a Vajrayana Root Guru so precious? Because Grandmaster Lu is an emanation body of buddhas.
chapterbody 4: Capable of being a teacher to humans and gods, teaching and transmitting all secret Buddhadharma, capable of "guiding," "redeeming," "clearing delusion," "instructing," "deliverance"...
chapterbody 5: All personal deities, dharma protectors, dakas, dakinis, and heavenly beings, appear only due to the teaching and guidance of the Root Guru.
chapterbody 6: You ought to view your Root Guru as a buddha. Abide by the "Fifty Stanzas of Guru Devotion."
chapterbody 7: However, there are indeed many who break the Root Guru vows and precepts. Hence, the number of cultivators who can truly reach accomplishment are like the stars at dawn, few and far between!
chapterbody 9: For example:
chapterbody 10: Devadatta was initially one of the eight princes who became a monk, and was one of the great disciples of Buddha. He was very close to Shakyamuni Buddha, and they were also relatives.
chapterbody 11: In the end he attacked the Buddha by accusing him of:
chapterbody 12: 1. living in a luxurious house (Bamboo Grove Monastery)
chapterbody 13: 2. wearing silken garments (the best dharma robes)
chapterbody 14: 3. eating fine food (receiving offerings)…
chapterbody 15: Devadatta developed doubts towards the Buddha. He wanted the position of leading the sangha, created conflict among the monks, and incited five hundred bhikkhus to leave the Buddha.
chapterbody 16: He broke his vows and precepts, and as a result, he descended to hell.
chapterbody 18: A second example:
chapterbody 19: The King of Tibet, Trisong Detsen, invited Padmasambhava to Tibet and took refuge under him. However, his confidence was weak and fragile.
chapterbody 20: In the beginning, the king's ministers who were believers in the Bon religion condemned the cultivation of Padmasambhava and Yeshe Tsogyal as:
chapterbody 21: 1. unlawful
chapterbody 22: 2. radical
chapterbody 23: 3. scamming
chapterbody 24: 4. effrontery
chapterbody 25: Consequently, King Trisong Detsen banished Padmasambhava and sent Yeshe Tsogyal into exile in "Ai Ba."
chapterbody 26: King Trisong Detsen also broke his vows and precepts.
chapterbody 27: Fortunately, he repented and cultivated Re-Purification, thus avoiding descending to the three lower realms.
chapterbody 31: Today, Grandmaster Lu, an emanation body of Amitabha Buddha, is similarly attacked by a woman. Anyone who has had contact with this woman knows that she is...
chapterbody 32: She defames Grandmaster Lu.
chapterbody 33: Inevitably breaking her vows and precepts to her Root Guru.
chapterbody 34: As for disciples affected by her, if they believe in this single-sided claim, lose faith and develop doubts, then they would have also broken their vows and precepts to their Root Guru.
chapterbody 35: Honoring one's Guru.
chapterbody 36: Treasuring Dharma.
chapterbody 37: Practicing diligently.
chapterbody 38: These will, of course, not be fulfilled, while the causes leading to the fate of descending to the three lower realms have instead sufficed!
chapterbody 42: I, Grandmaster Lu, vow that:
chapterbody 43: "If one honors one's Guru, treasures the Dharma, and practices diligently, yet doesn't become a buddha, I, Grandmaster Lu will bear responsibility!"
chapterbody 44: "I regard all sentient beings as myself, give rise to great bodhicitta, view sentient beings with equality, and benefit sentient beings."
chapterbody 45: "I never harm sentient beings."
chapterbody 46: "I have personally validated and understand the accomplishment of buddhahood, non-attachment to self and dharma. I teach the six paramitas and great compassion, the meditative cultivation of emptiness, freedom from worries and afflictions, neither birth nor death, the increase of kindness and virtue and the reduction of vice, the purification of body, speech, and mind."
chapterbody 47: It is necessary for holy disciples to armor themselves with Right Views and their own intuitive awareness of good and evil, so as to guard their Root Guru vows and precepts.

chaptername: 004 The Power of Contamination in the Fermentation Jar
chapterbody 2: "Staying far away from those who break precepts, and keeping a distance from evil-doers, is essential. This is because when one is in such company, one will definitely become polluted."
chapterbody 3: First of all, I had an objection towards these words. I said:
chapterbody 4: "For an enlightened person, shouldn't he view sentient beings in equality and without discrimination? Based on teachings regarding buddha-nature, those who break the precepts also have buddha-nature, evil-doers also have buddha-nature. How can we discriminate based on good or evil?"
chapterbody 6: "Only Grandmaster Lu views sentient beings in equality and doesn't discriminate. However, for a still-practicing cultivator, it is necessary to stay away from the corrupted."
chapterbody 8: A dharma propagator who breaks the precepts, will lead away a cultivation group.
chapterbody 9: A dharma propagator who breaks the precepts, will lead away a chapter.
chapterbody 10: A dharma propagator who breaks the precepts, will lead away a temple.
chapterbody 12: The answer lies in "the power of contamination."
chapterbody 16: Light finally dawned on Grandmaster Lu.
chapterbody 17: Why did Mencius's mother move house three times?
chapterbody 18: One who is near vermilion turns red.
chapterbody 19: One who is near ink becomes black.
chapterbody 21: I was reminded of Koreans' "kimchi fermenting culture." Their fermentation jars can be really, really massive. These jars contain sugar, salt, vinegar, food colorings, flavorings, tartness, sweetness, bitterness, spiciness, and so on.
chapterbody 22: Subsequently, all the vegetables are placed inside the jar.
chapterbody 23: The jar is buried in the ground.
chapterbody 24: When the jar is retrieved, the vegetables have become kimchi.
chapterbody 25: Not a single type of vegetable can retain its purity. Once it enters the fermentation jar, it absolutely becomes kimchi.
chapterbody 26: Such is the contaminating power of the fermentation jar; such a container is extremely fearsome. The saying that, "when you stay long enough in a fish market, you no longer become aware of its stench" makes sense.
chapterbody 27: Another saying:
chapterbody 28: Different cultures develop when ten miles apart.
chapterbody 29: Customary practices vary when five miles away.
chapterbody 30: These "customs" and "cultures" are all results caused by surrounding influences.
chapterbody 32: If we discover a dharma propagator who has gradually gone astray from lineage teachings, a dharma propagator who has become very conceited, a dharma propagator who has become extremely arrogant, a dharma propagator who believes he has great merit, a dharma propagator who disrespects his Root Guru, a dharma propagator who believes he is the greatest.
chapterbody 33: A dharma propagator who criticizes:
chapterbody 34: his/her Root Guru
chapterbody 35: Buddha
chapterbody 36: True Buddha Dharma
chapterbody 37: other dharma propagators.
chapterbody 38: You must remember, you must firmly bear in mind, that such a dharma propagator has already become shaky and unstable. With regard to "Honoring one's Guru," "Treasuring Dharma," and "Practicing diligently," this person has already broken his/her vows and precepts.
chapterbody 39: White Tara said:
chapterbody 40: "Swiftly keep a distance!"
chapterbody 42: Even if you, a cultivator, are wearing a clean and white robe, nonetheless, if you stay in a place of filthy mud, the dark murky water will surely contaminate your white robe and leave black stains.
chapterbody 43: Similarly, even if your own samaya is pure, you will inevitably be contaminated by others' damaged samaya, and become one of the jackals of the same lair.
chapterbody 44: In Vajrayana, if you have the slightest thread of doubt in your Root Guru, your Root Guru's Dharma, your Root Guru's lineage, and in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, you will not be able to attain the fruition of yogic union.
chapterbody 45: Be cautious! Be careful!
chapterbody 46: Remember firmly! Do not forget!
chapterbody 47: You must keep far away from those who break their vows and precepts!

chaptername: 005 Think Often that I am Bound to Die
chapterbody 2: "By frequently thinking that oneself will surely die, one can avoid behavior that breaks the vows and precepts."
chapterbody 4: "I have personally validated and realized the state of neither birth nor death, that birth and death originally do not exist; and have practiced and accomplished the Rainbow Light Yoga. Is there still a need to frequently think of death?"
chapterbody 6: "Grandmaster Lu's realization of neither birth nor death, that birth and death originally do not exist, is the unexcelled state of perfect enlightenment. Naturally it is a state which is spontaneous, unrestrained, and free of attachment. However, it is a different situation for most cultivators and ordinary people."
chapterbody 8: "General cultivators and ordinary people, all have attachments and things they cannot let go of. Due to these attachments that they cling to they then carry out negative actions that break their vows and precepts."
chapterbody 9: For example:
chapterbody 10: Avarice for money and property
chapterbody 11: Infatuation with women and sensual pleasures
chapterbody 12: Strife and antagonism to gain status
chapterbody 13: Enjoyment of gourmet cuisine
chapterbody 14: Attachment to high status and great wealth
chapterbody 15: These are the cravings and attachments of worldly people, forever insatiable. A person who practices cultivation, will also be subject to these influences, subsequently behaving in ways that break the precepts. Therefore, one should frequently think:
chapterbody 16: "I am bound to die!"
chapterbody 18: As life is too short, there is no need to put oneself in hardship to pursue worldly possessions. By avoiding the source of contamination, one will not be misled and fall into the three lower realms.
chapterbody 19: Keep firmly in mind that one is bound to die, and ultimately there is nothing that can be gained.
chapterbody 20: Keep firmly in mind that there will be infinite life in one's future, therefore seek liberation early.
chapterbody 22: In Taiwan, I have frequently mentioned:
chapterbody 23: "The idol of management, Wang Yung-ching - what did he gain? Also, President Chen Shui-bian - what did he gain? And there is Sayling Wen (Wen Shiren) - what did he gain as well?"
chapterbody 24: Wealth
chapterbody 25: Reputation
chapterbody 26: Beauty
chapterbody 27: In the end these are all empty in nature!
chapterbody 28: As cultivators, if we crave wealth and property, are infatuated by women and beauty, and fight for status, we are breaking the precepts.
chapterbody 29: We are bound to die! There is nothing that can be gained! We should abandon the strenuous and grievous pursuit of worldly pleasures. Otherwise, violating the precepts is inevitable, and falling into the three lower realms becomes inevitable!
chapterbody 30: We believe in karma, we know that no matter how minor a bad deed is, it will still lead to a negative effect.
chapterbody 32: A Samantabhadra Bodhisattva verse:
chapterbody 33: As days pass by, one's life is also spent.
chapterbody 34: Like fish in a pond of drying water, what fun is there?
chapterbody 38: Among dharma propagators in our school, many have already been spotted as being "greedy for money and property." These people know this very well in their hearts.
chapterbody 39: Their names have been listed in the , because they love money.
chapterbody 40: This is something that is very apparent!
chapterbody 41: After all, this also needs money, and that also requires money - once visitors enter inside that Lei Tsang temple, they will definitely be bled dry, such that they are barely able to escape intact. These people who love money, are indeed despicable!
chapterbody 43: I personally advocate:
chapterbody 44: "Everything should be in free will!"
chapterbody 45: The right path that is in accord with Dharma, is to have freewill donation!
chapterbody 46: Forcing people to donate, is going against the precepts!
chapterbody 47: To induce fear based on past life karma, also violates the precepts!
chapterbody 48: For some dharma propagators, their upholding of the precepts has already become damaged, yet they remain indifferent and apathetic; not only so, they continue to violate the precepts through their misdeeds, and sink deeper and deeper into the quagmire.
chapterbody 49: Here I advise:
chapterbody 50: "Frequently think that you are bound to die! Can you bring money with you? You ought to leave this dangerous situation immediately!"
chapterbody 51: Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms

chapterbody 2: "You should use your life to protect your vows and precepts! You should use your life to protect your vows and precepts! You should use your life to protect your vows and precepts!"
chapterbody 4: I was very surprised that this sentence was repeated three times. White Tara said:
chapterbody 5: "There is no need to be surprised! Vows and precepts are the root of liberation, and liberation is more important than one's life. So no matter what you do, it has to be in accord with Dharma, in harmony with one's vows and precepts, and only in this way can one accumulate 'resources,' 'merit,' and 'wisdom.'"
chapterbody 8: "There are three categories of people who will never be able to attain liberation. The first category of people are those who do not even want to be liberated. The second category of people are those who keep doing things that are not in accord with Dharma. The third category of people are those whose hearts are filled with the Five Poisons."
chapterbody 10: This sentence alarmed me, and reminded me of the analogy of the "three bowls":
chapterbody 11: 1. A covered or upturned bowl - the bowl is covered or upside down; a person in this category does not believe in the existence of buddhas, rebirth or reincarnation, or karma, and simply does not believe in anything. Even if dharma milk wanted to enter this bowl, a person in this category simply had no affinity. (Buddhas are unable to deliver those without affinity.)
chapterbody 12: 2. A broken bowl - the bowl has already been broken into pieces, or is chipped with a hole, or has a crack. This represents that this person's vows and precepts have already been damaged. Dharma milk was going to fill this bowl to the brim, but in the end it was lost through leakage; hence this bowl will never become full. People in this category are unable to attain liberation.
chapterbody 13: 3. A dirty bowl - this bowl is not broken or cracked; the only thing is that this bowl is dirty, it is not pure. When a dirty bowl is used to hold dharma milk, the result is that the dharma milk would also become contaminated. This represents that the hearts of people in this category are very filthy, full of craving, anger, ignorance, doubt, and arrogance. If one is not clean, one is also unable to attain liberation.
chapterbody 16: "Take refuge anew, immediately repent, and receive the precepts and vows once more. This is the dharma gate of Re-Purification."
chapterbody 17: A bowl that faces upward
chapterbody 18: A new bowl
chapterbody 19: A bowl that has been washed clean
chapterbody 20: (Only then will this be a suitable dharma vessel)
chapterbody 24: I, Grandmaster Lu, deeply understand White Tara's painstaking efforts, continuously helping me to deliver sentient beings.
chapterbody 25: White Tara has the greatest compassionate heart.
chapterbody 26: She hopes that those who have been listed in the Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms, would swiftly reflect on their actions.
chapterbody 27: And go on the right path afresh.
chapterbody 28: In a person's life, apart from the attainment and realization of buddhahood and benefiting sentient beings, everything else is meaningless.
chapterbody 29: Do not be attached to worldly concerns.
chapterbody 30: Worldly matters are the root of rebirth.
chapterbody 31: First practice in accord with Dharma -
chapterbody 32: Cultivate until one's illusory body manifests.
chapterbody 33: Cultivate until the rainbow light manifests.
chapterbody 34: Be fearless regarding death and the annihilation of the universe.
chapterbody 35: Know how to merge into the pure light.
chapterbody 36: Know how to realize the four holy states.
chapterbody 37: Know how to accomplish the perfect fruition of buddhahood.
chapterbody 38: Know how to become unfettered and at ease, and how to abide in spontaneous accomplishment.
chapterbody 42: My vow is: not to forsake a single sentient being. Naturally, I have countless dharma companions; it is inconceivable. Whether there are five million dharma companions, or one million, or a hundred thousand, or even just two, I know of course we are one body.
chapterbody 43: I would like to help my dharma companions, to share this inner awakening:
chapterbody 44: The practice of non-leakage
chapterbody 45: The practice of the arising of inner fire
chapterbody 46: The practice of light-drops
chapterbody 47: (The accomplishment of great bliss, the accomplishment of pure light, the accomplishment of the illusory body, the accomplishment of rainbow light, the accomplishment of no-death, the accomplishment of no-birth, the accomplishment of unfettered ease, spontaneous accomplishment, the accomplishment of Dzogchen, the Great Perfection)

chaptername: 007 The Precious Sword of the Yogi
chapterbody 2: "The precious sword of the yogi, is to protect one's vows and precepts. Only by protecting one's vows and precepts, can one cultivate in accord with Dharma. Daily life itself is cultivation, and this daily life is also contained within the vows and precepts."
chapterbody 4: White Tara explained:
chapterbody 6: We partake of meat, plants, and minerals.
chapterbody 8: The most important is the partaking of "qi."
chapterbody 9: (Before partaking of all that has life, we first make offerings, perform deliverance, and show our gratitude.)
chapterbody 12: Transforming the dharma robe that one wears into armor.
chapterbody 13: Or when wearing light clothing or unclothed, practice successfully to generate inner fire or the pure light, which is the real "clothing."
chapterbody 16: In all endeavors one must chant the secret mantras.
chapterbody 17: Purifying one's speech.
chapterbody 18: Engaging in gentle language, amiable language, kind language, truthful language.
chapterbody 19: Or remaining silent to allow the flow of energy.
chapterbody 20: All meaningless utterances should be avoided as much as possible.
chapterbody 21: Speech that breaks one's vows and precepts, should not be said at all. Speech is "mantra."
chapterbody 23: The cultivation of the "body":
chapterbody 24: Prostrating in Great Homage before the altar, prostrating in Great Homage to the Empty Space.
chapterbody 25: Circumambulating and paying homage to temples, stupas, and altars.
chapterbody 26: Purifying the cultivator's body.
chapterbody 27: The body has to be tranquil and at peace. The body is "purity."
chapterbody 29: The cultivation of the "mind":
chapterbody 30: The sensation of great bliss, the sensation of purity, the sensation of brilliant light, the sensation of sunyata (empty-nature).
chapterbody 31: The arising of the sensation of the Great Perfection.
chapterbody 32: Continuously abide in "meditative cultivation." "Meditative cultivation" is "mind."
chapterbody 34: The cultivation of "Dharma":
chapterbody 35: Be focused.
chapterbody 36: Practice the Preliminary Practices, Root Guru Yoga, Personal Deity Yoga, the practice of qi, channels and drops, the practice of non-leakage, inner fire, drops, the Heruka Practice, Highest Yoga Tantra, and Great Perfection Yoga.
chapterbody 37: Practice each Dharma accordingly, protect one's vows and precepts and the teachings.
chapterbody 38: Experience and validate them one by one. "Realization" itself is "Dharma."
chapterbody 40: The cultivation of "benefitting others":
chapterbody 41: Regard benefiting sentient beings as the first priority. Love all people and living things.
chapterbody 42: Rely upon Mahayana and give rise to great bodhicitta, cultivate "no-self." Do not be concerned only with yourself, but instead, benefit other people, help others.
chapterbody 44: The cultivation of "great compassion":
chapterbody 45: Regard your enemies as your parents and elders.
chapterbody 46: Love sentient beings the way you love yourself.
chapterbody 47: View all worldly things, such as houses, cars, jewelry, and money as manure.
chapterbody 48: Go help other people.
chapterbody 49: Give to others.
chapterbody 50: Being able to "let go" of all that is worldly, is "great compassion."
chapterbody 51: Every single person, from beginningless time, has gone through innumerable cycles of transmigration and rebirth, not only unable to attain accomplishment themselves, but also unable to help others reach accomplishment. Now that one has met Living Buddha Lian-sheng, Sheng-Yen Lu, one ought to take refuge, learn Buddhism well, and learn Dharma well. Otherwise, one will only become even more lost in the cycle of rebirth.

chapterbody 2: "When you do not understand a spiritual cultivator, or have merely heard rumors, or one-sided accounts, please do not slander casually. Unless you have already completely realized prajna, and you have the complete ability to know the thoughts of others, please do not casually denounce or disparage others."
chapterbody 3: "Slandering" is a prevalent illness afflicting worldly people.
chapterbody 4: The easiest precepts to break, are those pertaining to speech karma.
chapterbody 6: Worldly people easily fall prey to slandering others.
chapterbody 8: Spiritual cultivators easily commit the transgression of slandering other sects or schools of Buddhism.
chapterbody 9: Spiritual cultivators easily commit the transgression of self-praise while criticizing others.
chapterbody 10: Spiritual cultivators easily commit the transgression of picking on other cultivators' faults and delusions.
chapterbody 11: Spiritual cultivators slander other religious doctrines.
chapterbody 12: Spiritual cultivators with prejudices slander other religious teachers and their disciples due to ignorance or misunderstandings. Hence, it is very important to abandon "prejudices" and "attachments."
chapterbody 16: When I was in Taiwan, I came across a few books criticizing Esoteric Buddhism, especially the Tibetan schools of Esoteric Buddhism.
chapterbody 17: These books were written by a cultivator who was a student of the Consciousness-only (Yogachara) teachings. He even repudiated the patriarch of Esoteric Buddhism, Padmasambhava.
chapterbody 18: This spiritual cultivator also criticized Venerable Master Yin Shun from the Sanlun Sect.
chapterbody 19: He was of the opinion that:
chapterbody 20: Tibetan Esoteric Buddhism is "perversion."
chapterbody 21: Padmasambhava is "a demon of perversion."
chapterbody 22: Master Yin Shun firmly believed in and affirmed the six consciousnesses. As for the seventh consciousness and eighth consciousness, Master Yin Shun's belief that these were merely "nominal" was erroneous.
chapterbody 24: I would like to specially make an explanation here:
chapterbody 25: Padmasambhava had five female disciples who attained special accomplishments; all of them were Padmasambhava's dharma companions.
chapterbody 26: These five disciples were:
chapterbody 27: Yeshe Tsogyal
chapterbody 28: Mandarava
chapterbody 29: Belmo Sakya Devi
chapterbody 30: Belwong Kalasiddhi
chapterbody 31: Tashi Kyedren
chapterbody 32: These five disciples all attained great accomplishment.
chapterbody 33: Furthermore, it has been recorded in sutras and tantras that aside from these five, there were another fifteen who attained accomplishment.
chapterbody 34: In addition to these twenty yoginis, there were many more.
chapterbody 35: All of them were Padmasambhava's consorts.
chapterbody 36: Hence, Padmasambhava was criticized as a patriarch of perversion.
chapterbody 37: Tibetan Esoteric Buddhism as a school of perversion.
chapterbody 39: According to my understanding:
chapterbody 40: Padmasambhava's "Consort Practice" has its secret meaning, and belongs to the category of unsurpassed secrets. The cultivation of this practice aids the practitioner to attain "great bliss," "luminosity," "emptiness," and brings great benefit for the esoteric cultivation of qi, channels, and drops.
chapterbody 41: "Unblemished"
chapterbody 42: "Transcendental"
chapterbody 43: "Perfect and complete"
chapterbody 44: This kind of secret lineage is appropriate and correct; it is an integration of the generation and perfection stages, utilizing an expedient dharma gate to arrive at the transcendental and liberating wisdom of Tathagatas.
chapterbody 45: (This is something difficult for worldly people to understand, therefore it is always under criticism. Since the Buddhist practitioner who wrote those books had of course not cultivated and attained realization in this practice, inevitably it was hard to avoid slandering.)
chapterbody 46: Due to not knowing the secret meaning of this practice, thus committing the speech transgression of slandering, this cultivator had broken his vows and precepts.
chapterbody 47: This cultivator used Consciousness-only teachings to condemn other schools and sects of Buddhism; this is also committing the speech transgression of slander.
chapterbody 49: In my opinion:
chapterbody 50: There are far too many people in this world who have all committed the speech transgression of slandering, mainly because sentient beings who are blind number in the majority. There are sensationalism of the media, circulation of incorrect information through people's speech, and rumors by people with ill intent.
chapterbody 52: Consequently, false and inaccurate information becomes widespread in the world, while the real "truth" becomes submerged.
chapterbody 53: In this world, Buddhist practitioners should keep silent! Avoid breaking the precepts!

chaptername: 009 Ignorance is the Root of Reincarnation
chapterbody 2: "Since time immemorial, human beings have undergone innumerable reincarnations; however, they are still unable to end the cycles of transmigration, unable to accomplish the four holy states. When we examine the causes, the hindrance of 'ignorance' is the culprit."
chapterbody 3: White Tara's words touched me deeply.
chapterbody 4: Yes.
chapterbody 5: For many, many people, their lives follow a similar process:
chapterbody 6: Birth, kindergarten, primary school, junior high school, high school, university, postgraduate or master's studies, doctorate studies.
chapterbody 8: Marriage, starting a family, having children.
chapterbody 9: Marriage of sons and daughters, having grandchildren.
chapterbody 10: Old age, illness, death.
chapterbody 12: Therefore, Chen Chuan-Fang says:
chapterbody 13: At age one, one appears on scene.
chapterbody 14: At age ten, one is occupied in studies.
chapterbody 15: At age twenty, one is filled with billowing thoughts of love.
chapterbody 16: At age thirty, one strives in one's career.
chapterbody 17: At age forty, one puts on some weight.
chapterbody 18: At age fifty, one is old but vigorous.
chapterbody 19: At age sixty, one's blood pressure rises.
chapterbody 20: At age seventy, one starts becoming forgetful.
chapterbody 21: At age eighty, one becomes shaky. (Walks unsteadily)
chapterbody 22: At age ninety, one loses direction. (Memory loss)
chapterbody 23: At a hundred years of age, one is a portrait hung on the wall. (Death)
chapterbody 25: This is "human life." In general, few people are able to break away from this framework, this mode of living.
chapterbody 26: In this mode, everyone pursues worldly matters and objects relentlessly. Because of these relentless pursuits, "craving," "hatred," and "delusion" arise. Consequently, hearts are spurred to commit many negative deeds. The accumulation of negative karma, creates the cause for rebirth in the three lower realms.
chapterbody 27: White Tara said:
chapterbody 28: "This is ignorance!"
chapterbody 30: I said:
chapterbody 31: If a person has the opportunity to encounter Buddhadharma, then this is an extremely fortunate event indeed, because only Buddhadharma can lead you to exit the secular world, enter into the realm of sages and saints, liberate yourself from reincarnation, and reach the four holy states.
chapterbody 32: Although some people have already encountered Buddhadharma, and have also met great kind and wise friends, however, they are still unable to resist worldly temptations.
chapterbody 33: They associate with people who commit cumulative bad deeds.
chapterbody 34: Pursue status and reputation.
chapterbody 35: Crave sensual pleasures.
chapterbody 36: Hurt others.
chapterbody 37: Wrongfully seize others' wealth and property through force or power.
chapterbody 38: Kill living beings because of food cravings.
chapterbody 39: Covet luxurious residences, premium cars, jewelry.
chapterbody 41: White Tara said:
chapterbody 42: "These types of pursuits often incite one's heart to commit bad deeds, to seek relentlessly and tirelessly. It is a cycle of happiness and suffering, swinging between either gain or loss. Unable to let one's mind be at peace, this is also ignorance."
chapterbody 44: My personal opinion is:
chapterbody 45: A Buddhist practitioner should cultivate and uphold Buddhadharma single-mindedly.
chapterbody 46: Ultimately, the purification of one's body, speech, and mind is absolutely necessary.
chapterbody 47: One must detach oneself from the secular.
chapterbody 48: Think of ways for oneself to remedy one's worldly cravings.
chapterbody 49: Not only learning to remedy one's own cravings, but also influencing others to learn to remedy their cravings; this is bodhicitta.
chapterbody 50: Most importantly, not to have a selfish heart; frequently reflecting whether one is selfish.
chapterbody 52: Even more importantly, to love others more than oneself; this is the great compassionate heart.

chapterbody 2: "Sentient beings are inconceivable; a Buddhist practitioner needs to train his/her heart through sentient beings. Only by practicing compassion towards sentient beings and giving rise to bodhicitta, will one be able to accomplish the way of the Buddha. This is what is known as buddhas arising from the world."
chapterbody 3: I felt that these words were inconceivable.
chapterbody 4: I said:
chapterbody 5: "Sentient beings are not sentient beings and are sentient beings."
chapterbody 6: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 7: "This is something understood only by great enlightened ones, hence it is inconceivable. A Buddhist practitioner who aspires to attain bodhi, has to cultivate for the sake of sentient beings - everything for the sake of sentient beings. Only by such means will one be able to accomplish becoming a buddha or bodhisattva."
chapterbody 8: I said:
chapterbody 9: "If there are no sentient beings?"
chapterbody 10: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 11: "One is unable to become a buddha."
chapterbody 12: "Why?"
chapterbody 13: "Because there would be no cause to give rise to bodhicitta!"
chapterbody 17: Here, Grandmaster Lu would like to remind all spiritual cultivators.
chapterbody 18: Even if you have the wisdom of prajna, or possess the most powerful mantra, or the strength of a great deity, however, if there are no sentient beings, you are not able to give rise to bodhicitta, hence there is no means to reach accomplishment. Even if there is accomplishment, it would also be equivalent to zero.
chapterbody 19: This is a very remarkable thing.
chapterbody 21: I once said in a dharma talk:
chapterbody 22: "Grandmaster Lu is a great kind and wise friend with virtue, however, if there are no sentient beings who come and listen to Buddhadharma, what use is there?"
chapterbody 23: I might as well enter nirvana!
chapterbody 24: I might as well enter parinirvana!
chapterbody 25: To use a contemporary saying, "There would be no sense of accomplishment at all!"
chapterbody 27: Therefore, being dependent on sentient beings to give rise to bodhicitta is the key to becoming a buddha.
chapterbody 28: Because sentient beings exist, "troubles" and "worldliness" exist. A Buddhist practitioner who wants to remedy these troubles and worldliness will then practice meditative cultivation.
chapterbody 29: Because of the existence of sentient beings, a cultivator practices to rid oneself of all worldly "contaminants," stops climbing the social ladder and "seeking special relationships" in the secular world, consequently becoming a great yogi.
chapterbody 30: A Buddhist practitioner does not crave the eight worldly dharmas.
chapterbody 31: A Buddhist practitioner has to deliver sentient beings.
chapterbody 32: Fundamentally, a Buddhist practitioner recognizes that the emotive and sensory material world is also an illusion; we should not degenerate and sink in this illusion, but utilize the illusion of sentient beings to remedy afflictions and give rise to bodhicitta.
chapterbody 33: (This is a key point from White Tara.)
chapterbody 37: Maybe there are still many people who are unable to understand my explanation above, or think it is paradoxical.
chapterbody 38: In short, my message is:
chapterbody 39: A Buddhist practitioner is not apart from sentient beings. Yet remains unpolluted by sentient beings.
chapterbody 40: A Buddhist practitioner is not attached to the eight worldly concerns.
chapterbody 41: And gives rise to bodhicitta for the secular world.
chapterbody 42: A Buddhist practitioner has to deliver sentient beings.
chapterbody 43: The well-being and happiness of all sentient beings arise from the Buddha's teachings of Dharma.
chapterbody 44: Sentient beings have sorrows and joys.
chapterbody 45: Sentient beings can be good or evil.
chapterbody 46: Buddhist practitioners who cultivate amidst sentient beings have to guard and protect their own vows, aspirations, and samaya, abide by the precepts, and not become corrupted.
chapterbody 47: Only then can this be called "cultivation."
chapterbody 48: Personally, I understood White Tara's intent and key points, and the various relationships between a Buddhist practitioner and sentient beings.
chapterbody 49: Sentient beings can help a cultivator.
chapterbody 50: Sentient beings can also destroy a cultivator.
chapterbody 52: Sentient beings are truly inconceivable; the significance of my vow, not to abandon any sentient being, lies herein.

chaptername: 011 The Samaya Between a Buddhist Practitioner and Sentient Beings
chapterbody 2: Is there samaya between a Buddhist practitioner and sentient beings?
chapterbody 4: Yes.
chapterbody 5: Has Grandmaster Lu heard of this following verse?
chapterbody 6: Especially with regard to great wise and kind friends, their fundamental purpose is to free sentient beings from the cycles of transmigration. Sentient beings should take refuge in great wise and kind friends, as they can guide one in cultivation through Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.
chapterbody 8: As long as the empty space exists,
chapterbody 9: It will surely be filled with earth, water, fire, wind, and emptiness.
chapterbody 10: As long as earth, water, fire, wind, and emptiness still exist,
chapterbody 11: It will definitely be filled with sentient beings.
chapterbody 12: As long as sentient beings still exist,
chapterbody 13: Emotions and desires, cravings, hatred, and delusions will definitely be present everywhere.
chapterbody 14: As long as these Five Poisons still exist,
chapterbody 15: The great compassion of spiritual cultivators will definitely be present everywhere.
chapterbody 16: Within this, there is even more samaya!
chapterbody 17: Oh Grandmaster Lu! Grandmaster Lu, this is the samaya between a Buddhist practitioner and sentient beings.
chapterbody 19: I asked White Tara:
chapterbody 20: What kind of samaya does a Buddhist practitioner have towards sentient beings?
chapterbody 21: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 22: Like the sun, a Buddhist practitioner needs to light up the darkness of sentient beings.
chapterbody 23: Like one with clear vision, a Buddhist practitioner has to guide blind sentient beings.
chapterbody 24: Like one with great wealth, a Buddhist practitioner needs to relieve the poverty of sentient beings.
chapterbody 25: Like one who turns the dharma wheel, a Buddhist practitioner wants sentient beings to have spiritual refuge.
chapterbody 26: Like a teacher, a Buddhist practitioner wants sentient beings to learn Buddhadharma.
chapterbody 27: Like a vajra, a Buddhist practitioner has to protect sentient beings with their multitude of afflictions.
chapterbody 28: Like the great compassionate Guanyin, a Buddhist practitioner has to carry out the six paramitas of Buddhism, granting favors to sentient beings.
chapterbody 29: Like a bodhisattva, a Buddhist practitioner has to be able to benefit sentient beings.
chapterbody 30: Like Grandmaster Lu, a Buddhist practitioner has to not forsake a single sentient being, and teach Buddhadharma to all sentient beings equally and without discrimination, so that each and every sentient being can become a buddha.
chapterbody 31: This is samaya!
chapterbody 35: I asked White Tara:
chapterbody 36: Sentient beings are like the sea, able to keep a boat afloat, or capsize a boat. When sentient beings overturn the boat, what should a Buddhist practitioner do?
chapterbody 37: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 38: I will now teach practitioners how to cultivate; these are in fact turning points in a Buddhist practitioner's cultivation.
chapterbody 39: When sentient beings are poor,
chapterbody 40: this is the time and chance to teach Buddhist practitioners to "give."
chapterbody 41: When sentient beings insult you,
chapterbody 42: this is the time and chance to teach Buddhist practitioners to "patiently endure."
chapterbody 43: When sentient beings bully and oppress you,
chapterbody 44: this is the time and chance to teach Buddhist practitioners to "progress with diligence."
chapterbody 45: When sentient beings tempt Buddhist practitioners to commit bad deeds,
chapterbody 46: this is the time and chance to teach Buddhist practitioners to "abide by the precepts."
chapterbody 47: When sentient beings are blind, confused, and deluded,
chapterbody 48: this is the time and chance to teach Buddhist practitioners "wisdom."
chapterbody 49: When sentient beings are consumed by pipe dreams and wishful thinking,
chapterbody 50: this is the time and chance to teach Buddhist practitioners "meditative stability."

chapterbody 2: "Grandmaster Lu! Do you know the samaya of taking refuge?"
chapterbody 4: "Yes, it is written clearly on the certificate of taking refuge. After taking refuge in Buddha, one must not seek refuge in other gods or deities. After taking refuge in Dharma, one should learn Buddhadharma and not cause harm to any sentient being. After taking refuge in the Sangha, one should stop associating with heretics."
chapterbody 6: White Tara said:
chapterbody 7: "Grandmaster Lu! Do you know the true meaning of taking refuge?"
chapterbody 8: I said:
chapterbody 9: "Once one takes refuge in the Three Jewels, Buddha becomes one's great teacher, Dharma becomes one's path of cultivation, and the Sangha becomes one's dharma companion."
chapterbody 10: I added:
chapterbody 12: White Tara said:
chapterbody 13: "What is Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha?"
chapterbody 14: I said:
chapterbody 15: "Buddha is the awakened one, fully enlightened, beyond compare, worthy of the highest honor. Dharma refers to the teachings that the Buddha handed down, and only this lineage of dharma teachings can bring an end to the reincarnation of sentient beings and their transmigration through life and death. As for the Sangha, they are keepers of dharma teachings and are our companions in cultivation. Hence, the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha are our only object of refuge."
chapterbody 17: White Tara said:
chapterbody 18: "What you said is all correct. Then what is broken samaya?"
chapterbody 19: I said:
chapterbody 20: "After taking refuge, if one then goes and takes refuge in deviant paths, one's samaya becomes broken. After taking refuge, if one harms others, one's samaya becomes broken. After taking refuge, if one abandons the Three Jewels, one's samaya becomes broken. After taking refuge, if one gives up on cultivation because of worldly pursuits, one's samaya becomes broken. After taking refuge, if one disrespects the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, one's samaya becomes broken. After taking refuge, one must not abandon the Three Jewels regardless of the occurrences of favorable or adverse events."
chapterbody 22: White Tara asked:
chapterbody 23: "Do you take refuge only once?"
chapterbody 24: I answered:
chapterbody 25: "No. I take refuge all the time!"
chapterbody 26: White Tara asked:
chapterbody 27: "What do you mean by taking refuge all the time?"
chapterbody 28: I replied:
chapterbody 29: "This is a secret - every day I recite the Fourfold Refuge Mantra, think of the Three Jewels all the time, and reflect upon my refuge. I invoke the Three Jewels, pay great homage to the Three Jewels, make great offerings to the Three Jewels, praise the Three Jewels, and pray for the blessing of the Three Jewels, who are one's refuge all the way until buddhahood."
chapterbody 30: White Tara said:
chapterbody 31: "This is the correct way of taking refuge!"
chapterbody 33: White Tara asked:
chapterbody 34: "And who is your true refuge?"
chapterbody 35: I answered:
chapterbody 36: "This is highly secret. My true refuge, is self-refuge; refuge in my own buddha-nature."
chapterbody 37: I added: "I take refuge in the perfect and enlightened dharma body of buddha-nature; I take refuge in the superb and extraordinary tranquility of self-nature; I take refuge in all sages and saints who are of the same body, neither separate nor different."
chapterbody 39: White Tara then praised me:
chapterbody 40: Revered Lineage Root Guru,
chapterbody 41: By accomplishing your origin you become the yidam.
chapterbody 42: Bestowing dakinis with blessings,
chapterbody 43: Never departing from your three roots at all times.
chapterbody 45: White Tara said:
chapterbody 46: You have "seen" and validated buddha-nature.
chapterbody 47: You have "cultivated" into the realm of meditation.
chapterbody 48: You have "carried out" one refuge.
chapterbody 49: This is already the highest level of taking refuge. Buddhahood and reincarnation are one; this is beyond the limits of any language, beyond the confines of any category; this is the unexcelled state of perfect enlightenment.
chapterbody 50: This is indeed:
chapterbody 52: The most, most, most, most secret form of taking refuge.

chapterbody 2: "Enlightened buddhas also have samaya!"
chapterbody 4: "What is the samaya of buddhas?"
chapterbody 6: "Great compassion, the wisdom of emptiness, unfettered ease, spontaneous accomplishment, perfection."
chapterbody 8: I asked:
chapterbody 9: "When a Buddhist practitioner has already become enlightened, does an enlightened one still have samaya?"
chapterbody 10: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 12: An enlightened one completely understands "the mind is buddha," "buddha is mind," and "one's mind is buddha." The buddhas are not apart from oneself, one is not apart from buddhas. There is no difference between buddhas and oneself, the two are one.
chapterbody 13: Although enlightened ones already know that they are buddhas, they are still respectful towards their own Root Guru in whom they have taken refuge.
chapterbody 14: Rendering service.
chapterbody 16: Paying homage.
chapterbody 17: Praying for the blessing of their Root Guru.
chapterbody 18: (This is the samaya between an enlightened one and their Root Guru.)
chapterbody 20: An enlightened one has realized and understands that all phenomena arise from one's mind, and knows that when merit is not merit, it is indeed merit.
chapterbody 21: So-called merit is not having merit, and precisely because of the awareness that there is no merit, only then is it merit.
chapterbody 22: However, enlightened ones never stop accumulating the various causes and conditions of merit.
chapterbody 23: (This is the samaya between enlightened ones and merit.)
chapterbody 25: An enlightened one is fearless about everything, for example regarding reincarnation, descending to the three lower realms, being in dangerous situations, and so on...
chapterbody 26: They are clearly aware that reincarnation is illusory, the three lower realms are a dream, and that dangerous situations are also temporary.
chapterbody 27: However, enlightened ones will abandon even the slightest misdeeds. They would not carry out negative actions no matter how insignificant they seem.
chapterbody 28: (This is the samaya between enlightened ones and negative actions.)
chapterbody 30: Enlightened ones understand the unexcelled state of perfect enlightenment, and know that many pure lands are also born within oneself.
chapterbody 31: They know all of the dharma teachings pertaining to arhats, pratyekabuddhas, bodhisattvas, and buddhas.
chapterbody 32: They know the dharma teachings of Hinayana (Small Vehicle), Dviyana (Twofold Vehicle), Mahayana (Great Vehicle), and Vajrayana (Diamond Vehicle).
chapterbody 33: They know the dharma teachings of Great Perfection.
chapterbody 34: They know the dharma teachings pertaining to the Three Vehicles, Five Vehicles, and Nine Vehicles.
chapterbody 35: However, they do not disparage or vilify any of the dharma teachings.
chapterbody 36: (This is the samaya between enlightened ones and dharma teachings.)
chapterbody 38: Enlightened ones have already validated and know about many kinds of samadhi states. They are able to enter into samadhi and have ascertained the existence of siddhi.
chapterbody 39: Enlightened ones have attained accomplishment on the path of spiritual cultivation. They have validated each of the Ten Bhumis (Ten Stages of Bodhisattvahood).
chapterbody 40: However,
chapterbody 41: They are not complacent.
chapterbody 42: They do not boast.
chapterbody 43: They are not arrogant.
chapterbody 44: (This is the samaya between enlightened ones and the actualization of the path.)
chapterbody 46: Enlightened ones deeply understand the profound principle of equality without difference. They are fundamentally aware that there is no difference between oneself and others, no difference between oneself and sentient beings. I am a sentient being, and sentient beings are me. From the very beginning it has always been so; sentient beings and oneself are also a transient phenomenon.
chapterbody 47: However, enlightened ones cannot cease to have a great compassionate heart towards sentient beings.
chapterbody 48: (This is the samaya between enlightened ones and sentient beings.)
chapterbody 50: Enlightened ones already understand that the two phenomena of reincarnation and nirvana are one in its nature and not two. Hence, samsara is nirvana, and nirvana is samsara; afflictions are bodhi, and bodhi is afflictions.
chapterbody 52: However, enlightened ones never stop progressing along "the path of cultivation."
chapterbody 53: (This is the samaya between enlightened ones and cultivation.)

chapterbody 2: "Actual practice of Buddhadharma is diligent and unremitting, like the flowing water of a great river streaming continuously into the boundless sea."
chapterbody 4: "Grandmaster Lu is number one in diligence."
chapterbody 5: As a person, I don't really have any strong points. However, when it comes to perseverance, I would rank myself first.
chapterbody 6: Regarding writing, I write one article every day, unceasingly.
chapterbody 7: Regarding cultivation, I practice dharma every day, unceasingly.
chapterbody 8: Regarding painting, I paint a picture every day, unceasingly.
chapterbody 9: Regarding reading, I read every day, unceasingly.
chapterbody 10: Regarding samadhi, I practice meditation every day, unceasingly.
chapterbody 11: I said, "No matter whether a Buddhist patriarch is of the Zen, Pure Land, Tantric, or Vinaya school, if I walk past their place of retreat, they would rise and applaud, Sheng-Yen Lu is number one in diligence."
chapterbody 12: In fact, I am not a "workaholic," I just abide in the luminosity of enlightenment and progress vividly, lucidly, and alertly.
chapterbody 13: I am merely completing my homework for each day!
chapterbody 14: White Tara said:
chapterbody 15: "To find someone like you in this world has now become very difficult!"
chapterbody 16: I replied:
chapterbody 17: "I am just doing my best, and I know nothing else."
chapterbody 19: White Tara said:
chapterbody 20: In this world, there are many Buddhist practitioners who, after reaching a particular meditation state, stop cultivating further and continue to stay permanently in that state. They do not know that such a meditation state is just temporary.
chapterbody 21: (This breaks the samaya of diligence.)
chapterbody 23: For example:
chapterbody 24: When one practices qi, channels, and drops, one develops a craving for the sensation of great bliss arising from "qi passing through channels," and "inner fire passing through channels." Such a sensation is indeed very mesmerizing, but this is only the blissful pleasure samadhi state of the Heaven of the Desire Realm.
chapterbody 25: If one becomes attached to this pleasure,
chapterbody 26: And ceases to progress,
chapterbody 27: Then one can only remain in the heavens of the Realm of Desire!
chapterbody 29: Likewise:
chapterbody 30: From the samadhi of great bliss, a cultivator develops the "samadhi of clear light" where one's body, speech, and mind have reached the accomplishment of infinite light, illuminating heaven and earth. Subsequently, one believes that the "samadhi of clear light" is the end of cultivation. This is also a very mesmerizing state.
chapterbody 31: This is the samadhi state of the "Heaven of the Realm of Form."
chapterbody 32: If one becomes attached to clear light,
chapterbody 33: And does not think of progressing,
chapterbody 34: Then one can only remain in the heavens of the Realm of Form!
chapterbody 36: Likewise:
chapterbody 37: From the "samadhi of clear light," a cultivator develops "emptiness," from clear light one enters the "four realms of emptiness." These samadhi states are: 1) infinity of space (akasanantya), 2) infinity of consciousness (vijñananantya), 3) nothingness (akiñcanya), and 4) neither thought nor non-thought (naivasaṃjñānasaṃjñā).
chapterbody 38: If one thinks that one has reached the end,
chapterbody 39: And does not think of progressing,
chapterbody 40: Then one can only remain in the heavens of the Realm of Formlessness!
chapterbody 44: White Tara said:
chapterbody 45: "These are all sensations arising from the accomplishment of great bliss. When one experiences these sensations of bliss, clear light, and emptiness, one ought to know that these are all temporary meditation states, and should not think that one is amazing and becomes complacent. If one thinks this way, one has broken the samaya of diligence!"
chapterbody 46: White Tara continued:
chapterbody 47: "Cultivators of the present age have neither attained nor validated the meditation states of bliss, clear light, and emptiness. Instead, they have attained the states of irritability, drowsiness, and sluggishness, and cease to progress any further. This is even worse than before."
chapterbody 48: I said:
chapterbody 49: "When a yogi has cultivated to the state of non-cultivation, actually, even a yogi's non-cultivation is also cultivation."

chapterbody 2: "There are many Buddhist practitioners, who have not yet attained but claim they have attained, who have not yet realized but claim they have realized; these are all grandiose exaggerations. Grandmaster Lu, you know that this is a violation of the precepts, these false overstatements break the samaya of speech!"
chapterbody 3: When I heard this, I sweated! Because in the Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms, those who exaggerate and make false statements are far too many. Therefore, only by finding and following a master who has true virtue, will one be able to avoid falling easily into the clutches of mara.
chapterbody 5: There is a "Braggadocio Dharma King" who is so extremely full of bluster and hot air, that it could be said, there was nothing he did not exaggerate. It was simply too much!
chapterbody 6: For instance:
chapterbody 8: "Adi-buddha (the primordial buddha) manifested and called me his father. He paid homage to me, made offerings, and prayed for my blessing. Indeed, I am the unsurpassed buddha who has been in existence before him."
chapterbody 9: (In fact, the Braggadocio Dharma King did not really know much about Buddhadharma.)
chapterbody 12: "My supernatural abilities are unexcelled, and the universe was created by me. With limitless spiritual power, the Five Dhyani Buddhas and even innumerable buddhas were all taught and trained by me so that they could become buddhas."
chapterbody 13: (In fact, the Braggadocio Dharma King has never cultivated any dharma practices.)
chapterbody 16: "Grandmaster Lu is just a person with a little bit of supernatural power, and it was I who gave him empowerment. A mere puff of my breath, and he will die. He ought to come and take refuge in me. The reason he is still alive is due to my compassionate heart. After all, there is no need to overexert myself over something so effortless. With just one thought of mine, and he will enter nirvana!"
chapterbody 18: The Braggadocio Dharma King said:
chapterbody 19: "Although Padmasambhava, Nagarjuna, Virupa, Naropa, and Tsongkhapa wished to become my disciples, I actually refused their requests. Their natural capacities are all too inferior, and I attained the fruition of buddhahood earlier than them. Unlike them, I did not need to practice meditation, and had already become buddha, whereas they all needed to practice meditation before they could become buddhas."
chapterbody 20: (To be able to become a buddha without meditation is indeed strange!)
chapterbody 22: The Braggadocio Dharma King said:
chapterbody 23: "I am one who does not need dharma lineage, as I am the origin of the lineage. Below me, masters of the Nyingma, Gelugpa, Kagyu, and Sakya sects are all my disciples and disciples' disciples. True Buddha School's Grandmaster Lu is a distant disciple of mine, and he ought to come and serve me."
chapterbody 24: (To claim accomplishment in the absence of dharma lineage is an excessively grandiose exaggeration.)
chapterbody 26: The Braggadocio Dharma King said:
chapterbody 27: "I am one who does not need Buddhadharma, as I am above Buddhadharma. I do not need Dharma teachings as that is only necessary for those with inferior capacities. I do not need to follow precepts, as I am above precepts. Since the very beginning, I have already attained liberation and enlightenment. Buddhadharma and precepts are all just garbage in my eyes."
chapterbody 28: (No Buddhadharma, no precepts. Sutras, vinaya, and treatises are all rendered void by him!)
chapterbody 30: The Braggadocio Dharma King said:
chapterbody 31: "Learn liberation from me, and you don't need to practice any dharma. You only need my blessing power, and you can quickly become a buddha. Even if you are in the three lower realms, if I call out your name, you will be liberated and become a buddha. As for hell, it is within the nail of my little finger, all I need is a mere snap of my fingers."
chapterbody 33: The Braggadocio Dharma King said:
chapterbody 34: "There is no need for the six paramitas, no need for effort and diligence. The key to accomplishment or not, is wholly dependent on me, the Dharma King. There is no need to go through tribulations. If I think about you, you will become liberated."
chapterbody 36: The Braggadocio Dharma King said:
chapterbody 37: "The path of liberation is just a name, the path of bodhi is just a name. What is real is my blessing empowerment."
chapterbody 38: Oddly enough, while some people were stunned or taken aback by these claims of the Braggadocio Dharma King, there were also a group of others who actually believed him.
chapterbody 40: However, real Buddhist practitioners must firmly remember:
chapterbody 41: Listen to Dharma teachings.
chapterbody 42: Understand Buddhadharma.
chapterbody 43: Practice Dharma with single-minded focus.
chapterbody 44: Obtain lineage empowerment.
chapterbody 45: Abide by and uphold precepts.
chapterbody 46: Cultivate meditation and validate enlightenment.

chaptername: 016 When a Blind Cat Meets a Dead Rat
chapterbody 2: "Among the faults of cultivators, one of them is the self-proclamation of one's merit. There is actually no such thing, however, for reputation or benefit a cultivator self-proclaims merit. Over time this becomes a habit, and one has broken one's samaya through false speech!"
chapterbody 4: I asked:
chapterbody 6: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 7: "First example - a person just passed away and his/her relatives come to inform a cultivator. The cultivator promptly says that during the previous night, the soul of the deceased had already come, and the cultivator had already specially conducted a bardo deliverance, so that the soul of the deceased was now already at the pure land of the Western Paradise. Upon hearing this, relatives of the deceased hand over a generous offering out of gratitude; they are also moved, and subsequently allow the cultivator to hold sway over them."
chapterbody 8: (Actually, the deliverance event never happened at all.)
chapterbody 10: White Tara continued:
chapterbody 11: "Second example - a married couple who have been childless for a long time, talk to a cultivator about their situation. The cultivator says that he/she will give them a blessing, and later on the couple really have a child. The cultivator claims that he/she had carried down this child from heaven, as a gift to the married couple. The parents of the child are deeply grateful and tearful, and the cultivator is of course offered another generous red envelope and treated with respect like a deity."
chapterbody 12: (Actually, there was no such thing, as it was just a coincidence!)
chapterbody 14: White Tara continued:
chapterbody 15: "Third example - a person fell sick for a long time but had not yet recovered, came to talk to the cultivator, and later recovers. The cultivator then claims that he/she had travelled to the heavenly realms, and specially invited a celestial physician to come and treat the person's illness. Now that the person has indeed recovered, it is yet another instance of great merit."
chapterbody 16: (Actually, there was no such thing at all.)
chapterbody 18: White Tara said:
chapterbody 19: "Fourth example - a cultivator makes a prediction that a person will die within six months. This person becomes fearful and asks for help from the cultivator. The cultivator promises to offer protection. Six months later, this person is still alive and the cultivator claims that it was because he/she had invoked the help of dharma protectors and guardian deities to look after the person at all times."
chapterbody 20: (Actually, the prediction was a fake, and the protection was also non-existent.)
chapterbody 22: White Tara said:
chapterbody 23: "Fifth example - a cultivator self-proclaims to have knowledge of karmic links between past, present, and future lives. Whenever anyone approaches, the cultivator would tell them, in previous lives I was your teacher, and you vowed to follow me life after life, hence in this life you have to continue to follow me. Consequently, these people allowed the cultivator to hold sway over them."
chapterbody 24: (In fact, the cultivator had absolutely no knowledge of karmic links between past, present, and future lives.)
chapterbody 26: White Tara said:
chapterbody 27: "Sixth example - a cultivator self-proclaims to have the ability to travel spiritually to heaven and hell, when in fact he/she has never ever done so. He/she self-proclaims to have the ability to give orders and instructions to spirits and deities, when in fact, spirits and deities often secretly make fun of him/her."
chapterbody 28: (The cultivator did not have sufficient spiritual power at all.)
chapterbody 30: White Tara said:
chapterbody 31: "Seventh example - a cultivator proclaims that a dragon deity emerged from a lake and paid homage to the cultivator. Whenever this cultivator goes to a temple, buddhas and deities of the temple would line up to welcome him/her. Wherever he/she goes, mountain and earth deities would all make offerings to him/her."
chapterbody 32: (Basically, false exaggeration.)
chapterbody 34: White Tara said:
chapterbody 35: "A cultivator self-proclaims to have participated in a meeting with buddhas, to discuss how to save the earth, and deliver sentient beings. In this meeting to save the human race, he/she was the only representative there on behalf of all other Buddhist practitioners in the world."
chapterbody 36: (Utter nonsense.)
chapterbody 38: White Tara said:
chapterbody 39: "A cultivator self-proclaims that celestial beings in the heavens all regard and rely upon him/her as a teacher, a teacher of devas and human beings. The Lords of the Netherworld and the officials of hell also listen to and obey his/her command. He/she claims that he/she is the unsurpassed and most worthy one in the universe!"
chapterbody 40: (It is still early!)
chapterbody 42: White Tara said:
chapterbody 43: "There are many cultivators of this sort. All of them are listed in the Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms. Through exaggerations and lies, they engage in false speech repeatedly, and over time, this naturally becomes a habit. For the sake of reputation and benefit, they commit one transgression after another, which will prevent them from leaving suffering and liberating themselves!"
chapterbody 44: Such behaviors should be given up!
chapterbody 45: Abide by the precepts!

chapterbody 2: "The first important precept for a Buddhist practitioner is not to kill. The five heinous crimes are the most severe transgressions; they are also the most difficult to resolve. For example, killing one's father, mother, killing an arhat, causing a buddha to bleed, and killing a sage or a monk. The karma resulting from these acts of killing is very difficult to purify, hence, these are called the five heinous crimes."
chapterbody 4: "What about the karmic consequence of creating a schism in the Sangha?"
chapterbody 6: "The Sangha refers to monastic communities of monks and nuns who practice in harmony. Causing discord or strife in the Sangha, is equivalent to killing the wisdom-lives of the assemblage of monks and nuns. The karmic consequence for this sin is extremely severe, and is equivalent to that resulting from the five heinous crimes, leading to endless torture in the Hell of Uninterrupted Suffering (Avici Hell). The length of time one must suffer in this hell is infinite."
chapterbody 7: (The sins listed above are the most severe karmic transgressions!)
chapterbody 9: White Tara said:
chapterbody 10: "No matter what is said, the crime of killing is listed as the number one sin which results in falling into the three lower realms, and it is the number one heaviest sin. A cultivator must take note not to commit three kinds of killing. First, killing arising from greed or craving - when one craves the flesh or meat of animals, desires their skins, or wishes to obtain benefits from their death. Second, killing arising from anger - when one commits murder due to envy, jealousy, hatred or resentment, over money or lust. Third, killing arising from ignorance - for example, destroying nests of birds, beehives, ant nests and so on."
chapterbody 12: "Evil intent, the plotting of schemes or assassinations, actual killing and being without remorse; these crimes cannot be absolved!"
chapterbody 16: The words of White Tara led me to think of the Great Khan of Mongolia, Genghis Khan (Temujin). Genghis Khan's army was brave and battlewise, and he established the Mongol empire.
chapterbody 17: At the time when Genghis Khan was attacking India, he remembered that in northern China, there was a Taoist master Qiu Chang-chun.
chapterbody 18: At the Indian border, Genghis Khan invited Qiu Chang-chun to meet him there, so that he could consult him about Tao.
chapterbody 19: Qiu Chang-chun traveled day and night to the Indian border.
chapterbody 20: Genghis Khan asked:
chapterbody 21: "What is Tao?"
chapterbody 22: Taoist master Qiu Chang-chun only answered:
chapterbody 23: "To kill less people!"
chapterbody 24: Genghis Khan and Taoist master Qiu Chang-chun made an agreement involving a Copper Symbol and Iron Seal. Later, Genghis Khan invaded China. The residents of northern China pasted the Copper Symbol of Qiu Chang-chun on their doors. As long as they did so, Mongolian troops would not break in and kill the residents. Thus, many lives were saved.
chapterbody 25: I would say:
chapterbody 26: Genghis Khan
chapterbody 27: Qin Shi Huang
chapterbody 28: Emperor Wu of Han
chapterbody 29: Alexander the Great
chapterbody 30: Julius Caesar…
chapterbody 31: All of them killed countless people. Having deep and heavy sins, they suffer in hell for inexorable, endless durations of time. Even until now, they are still in hell, tormented by excruciating agony!
chapterbody 35: Tantric practitioners cultivate qi, channels, and drops in their practice; qi and inner fire both utilize nutrition derived from the meat of animals.
chapterbody 36: Tantric practitioners also know that one should not kill.
chapterbody 37: Therefore, before drinking and eating, they practice a method of spiritual deliverance.
chapterbody 38: It is necessary to send the spirits of animals that will be eaten to higher realms through the power of mantra, mudra and visualization. (By doing so, there will be no negative karma.)
chapterbody 40: Buddha once said:
chapterbody 41: A mouthful of clean water,
chapterbody 42: Contains 84,000 organisms.
chapterbody 43: If one does not chant mantra,
chapterbody 44: It is equivalent to killing living beings.

chapterbody 2: "Seizing other people's money or property as one's own, whether through violent robbery, or covert theft, or through trickery and deceit - all of these violate Buddhist precepts. Most offenders of this precept will be reborn in the Realm of Hungry Ghosts."
