chapterbody 8: Disciples thought that their offerings would really be forwarded to Grandmaster Lu.
chapterbody 9: Actually, all of the offerings had been taken by this dharma propagator.
chapterbody 10: Until one day, disciples asked me, had I received their offerings?
chapterbody 12: The deception of this dharma propagator was thus exposed.
chapterbody 14: In the past:
chapterbody 15: There was yet another dharma propagator who swindled a lot of money from a temple, using the pretext of offering it to Grandmaster Lu.
chapterbody 16: In fact, the dharma propagator had misappropriated all the money.
chapterbody 17: Later, when a temple staff member informed me of this matter.
chapterbody 18: We realized that we had been fooled again!…
chapterbody 20: On another occasion, a distinguished guest came from a distant city. This distinguished guest was of great importance to the future of our True Buddha School.
chapterbody 21: We received and attended to him with great courtesy.
chapterbody 22: However, someone stole his wallet when he was listening to my dharma teaching.
chapterbody 23: He said:
chapterbody 24: "There are thieves in your temple!" I sweated.
chapterbody 25: I would like to ask the thief:
chapterbody 26: What kind of dharma teachings are you listening to?
chapterbody 30: People of ignorance
chapterbody 31: People without conscience
chapterbody 32: People without morals
chapterbody 33: People without shame
chapterbody 34: Only those such as these will commit robbery, theft and fraud.
chapterbody 35: As Buddhist practitioners, we clearly know that not to steal is the second important precept of the Five Precepts; this precept is absolutely not to be violated. Even highly suspicious behavior of "putting on your shoes in a melon patch or adjusting your cap under a plum tree" is to be avoided, let alone breaking such an important precept. Those who perform such acts of theft, are not carrying out spiritual cultivation, instead they are carrying out bad deeds, and their names are listed in the
chapterbody 37: I, Grandmaster Lu, would say:
chapterbody 38: "All who are greedy for money, you must be self-aware. In the quiet of the night, reflect upon your conscience - have you overdone it? Have you taken what you should not take? Have you been too greedy? Are you still a cultivating practitioner?"
chapterbody 39: Do not deceive yourself.
chapterbody 40: Do not deceive others.
chapterbody 41: If you deceive yourself and others, you will fall into the three lower realms; your name is already on the list.
chapterbody 43: White Tara said:
chapterbody 44: "Those who commit the crime of theft will fall into the Realm of Hungry Ghosts. Even if they are reborn as human beings, they will be poor and penniless their whole lives. In particular, this bad habit will cause you to commit the crime of theft in many of your successive lives."
chapterbody 45: I said:
chapterbody 46: "This kind of misconduct is something that even some dharma propagators can commit. We do not know what kind of dharma you are teaching?"
chapterbody 47: I said:
chapterbody 48: "All disciples should utilize their wisdom-eyes to watch those dharma propagators who have habitual tendencies of being too greedy for money!"
chapterbody 51: Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms.

chapterbody 2: "Male and female intercourse is sex. If it is in keeping with Buddhist precepts, it is ethical, otherwise it is sexual misconduct. Ethical sex is normal, while sexual misconduct is improper. Sexual misconduct will cause one to fall into the animal realm."
chapterbody 4: "All cultivators who are monastics must refrain from sex. Even ethical sex is not allowed. Vajrayana Buddhist monks are single-body practitioners, totally different from householders. Whether one is a monk or a nun, it is the same - if a monk or a nun engages in sex, he or she will definitely fall into the animal realm."
chapterbody 6: White Tara added:
chapterbody 7: "Vajrayana Buddhism has the Consort Practice, and Taoism also has a Dual Cultivation Practice. These practices are the most esoteric dharma teachings. If practitioners with the highest capacities practice in accord with dharma, this is not wrong. However, if there is anything in the slightest that is not in keeping with dharma, it will result in degeneration. Hence, practitioners who attain accomplishment through this path are very rare. Only a true vajra master can practice this. Otherwise, one will undoubtedly fall into the Hell of Uninterrupted Suffering (Avici Hell). Following this, one will reincarnate for many lifetimes in the animal realm."
chapterbody 8: (This article will not talk about the Consort Practice, nor the Dual Cultivation Practice. Instead it will only discuss what is sexual misconduct.)

chapterbody 26: From what I know:

chapterbody 30: "Sex" is also the reason humankind continues to survive.

chaptername: 020 Shooting Arrows Out of Thin Air
chapterbody 2: "The transgression of unkind speech is easy to commit. If one gives a false account of the truth, whether through denying the truth, or fabricating a story, or simply speaking nonsense, it will become a habit after a while. This behavior breaks the samaya of speech, and it causes negative karma and great troubles arise from such."
chapterbody 4: "Monks do not lie!"
chapterbody 5: Actually, it is not only monks who should not lie, even lay people should not either.
chapterbody 6: In reality, many people lie.
chapterbody 7: In this world, the possibility of finding someone who really tells the "truth" is almost close to "zero."
chapterbody 8: Everyone is: "shooting arrows out of thin air"!
chapterbody 12: For example:
chapterbody 13: Someone concocted a whole series of stories to defame Grandmaster Lu.
chapterbody 14: Others read it, and they spread like wildfire causing a furor around the world.
chapterbody 15: This media company decided to publish it. That media company also decided to publish it.
chapterbody 16: Various media sources all published these defamatory stories.
chapterbody 17: It seemed as if Grandmaster Lu had become the most heinous, unpardonable, wicked and atrocious sinner.
chapterbody 18: However, in fact, this is not the case.
chapterbody 19: This is an example of distorting the truth in speech!
chapterbody 20: We as spiritual practitioners neither wish to answer, nor engage in debate.
chapterbody 21: I kept thinking.
chapterbody 22: "If these stories are true, then I should correct my mistakes and become better. However, if these stories are false and full of nonsense, then there is no need to let it bother me."
chapterbody 23: (Because such is the human world.)

chapterbody 35: Awkward silence.…

chapterbody 41: 2. beneficial and harmless
chapterbody 42: 3. non-beneficial and harmful
chapterbody 43: 4. beneficial as well as harmful
chapterbody 44: Moreover, I have mentioned before that some spiritual practitioners claim they have great supernatural power, when in fact they do not have any supernatural power at all.
chapterbody 45: (This is also false speech.)

chapterbody 48: "Do not shoot arrows out of thin air! Speaking falsely has its karmic effect. One will fall into the evil paths, and in future lives, people will not believe what one says. In future lives, one will lie again and again, and over time this will become a habit."
chapterbody 49: I feel that the parable of "The Wolf is Coming!" teaches a lesson to liars.

chapterbody 51: This time, all the villagers did not run away after they heard "The wolf is coming!"

chapterbody 2: "There is a category of people, whose lips never stop moving, and everything they say is unkind. They specialize in causing harm to others through their gossip, tattling left and right, about this and that, and all these stories result in great troubles."
chapterbody 4: "When one hurts others with words - one has a poisonous tongue. In the future, such people will fall into the three lower realms and transmigrate to become venomous snakes. When people of this category fall into the three lower realms, they may also become venomous beasts, venomous birds, or other venomous creatures. Even if they are able to be reborn as human beings, they will engage in endless arguments and take delight in duplicity."
chapterbody 5: We often hear about the following:
chapterbody 6: People who frequently drop in on others' homes and take pleasure in chatting about the long and shortcomings of others.
chapterbody 7: Gossipmongers who specialize in discussing the right and wrong doings of other people.
chapterbody 8: People who drive a wedge between good friends. Two-faced scoundrels.
chapterbody 9: Two parties both claiming to be in the right.
chapterbody 10: People in this category have a great pile of justifications, but in truth, these are all "skewed reasoning," causing harm to countless others.
chapterbody 11: In fact, troubles caused by poisonous tongues may not be trivial. They could be enormous enough to trigger wars between two countries.
chapterbody 12: Wars between clans
chapterbody 13: Wars between races or nations
chapterbody 14: Wars within families
chapterbody 17: In religion - Devadatta's "poisonous tongue" was the most destructive. He detailed all the shortcomings in the Buddha's life and exaggerated them widely. He spread these lies in the Sangha. Consequently, he caused a schism in the Sangha, breaking the monastic community apart into two. Half of the Sangha who still had firm confidence in the Buddha followed the Buddha. The other half of the Sangha, who believed in what Devadatta said, left the Buddha and followed Devadatta. The Sangha was split asunder. This was one of the Buddha's Ten Worries, the greatest worry of the Buddha at that time.
chapterbody 18: In our True Buddha School, there are also inevitably such people who fish in troubled waters. Gossiping about this and that. On the surface, they appear to be True Buddha disciples, in actual fact however they create disharmony among disciples. Wise people should observe closely and listen carefully.
chapterbody 19: Who is causing rifts within the Sangha?
chapterbody 20: Who is causing discord among disciples?
chapterbody 21: Who is creating small private cliques?
chapterbody 22: It is very apparent.
chapterbody 23: (It is only because Grandmaster Lu has always commended the good and disregarded the faults of others, that I do not wish to disclose who these people are. However, disciples should keep their eyes wide open.)
chapterbody 27: White Tara said:
chapterbody 28: "Speak kind words."
chapterbody 29: She continued:
chapterbody 30: "Silence is golden. One should learn to speak less, and recite Buddha epithets and mantras more."
chapterbody 31: White Tara said:
chapterbody 32: "Refrain from talking."
chapterbody 34: I observed:
chapterbody 35: 1. Some people openly cause discord.
chapterbody 36: 2. Some people sow dissension in an indirect and circuitous manner.
chapterbody 37: 3. Some people cause alienation through private whisperings.
chapterbody 38: These three kinds all fall into the category of causing discord. All of them are equally guilty in creating negative speech karma. A dharma propagator who has the shortcoming of a "poisonous tongue" will definitely fall into the three lower realms. Why not search your own heart and ask yourself:
chapterbody 39: "Do I have a poisonous tongue?"
chapterbody 40: If so, repent swiftly, and be cleansed through "Re-Purification Dharma."
chapterbody 41: It is extremely easy to commit the fault of having a "poisonous tongue" to the extent that there are even people who derive pleasure from having "poisonous tongues." This is a most undesirable habit, as causing harm to others is equivalent to causing harm to oneself - it is indeed to be dreaded!

chapterbody 2: "Unkind speech includes loose speech, and loose speech has three meanings. One, meaningless talk. Two, bawdy jokes. Three, endless idle chatter."
chapterbody 4: I would say:
chapterbody 5: "Buddhist cultivators should observe golden silence, and instead practice more inner-observation and mindfulness, because when there is excessive talking, it becomes meaningless chitchat. Firstly, it is a waste of precious time; secondly, too much talking inevitably leads to slips of the tongue. Thirdly, loose lips are often a cause of troubles. Fourthly, bawdy jokes are also a violation of samaya."
chapterbody 6: I often hear people say:
chapterbody 7: We talked for three days and three nights.
chapterbody 8: We chatted all night until daybreak.
chapterbody 9: Bored people look for other bored people to chat with. They talk and talk, but in the end they still remain bored.
chapterbody 10: Many people like to chitchat.
chapterbody 11: Many people like to talk frivolously and endlessly.
chapterbody 12: White Tara said:
chapterbody 13: "It is better to utter less of insignificant words, idle chatter, and irrelevant comments, as wasting one's time and life will cause a person to be reborn in the three lower realms, and become cicadas, frogs, crickets and so on, incessantly croaking and chirping."
chapterbody 14: "Chitchat has become a habit for people, who derive enjoyment from casual conversations."
chapterbody 15: "For cultivators, delighting in meaningless and casual conversations not only compromises the recitation of Buddhas' epithets and mantras, inner-observation and mindfulness, meditation, diligence, and wisdom, it also causes cultivators to more easily break the samaya of speech."
chapterbody 17: I feel deeply that:
chapterbody 18: Human life is already very brief and fleeting as it is, and there is often not enough time. Moreover, people are all rushing about striving in their careers and businesses, such that the remaining amount of time left is barely sufficient for cultivation.
chapterbody 19: If time is spent on:
chapterbody 20: Chatting
chapterbody 21: Long-drawn-out and frivolous conversations
chapterbody 22: Meaningless talk
chapterbody 23: Wouldn't it be a waste?
chapterbody 27: Someone asked me:
chapterbody 28: "Grandmaster Lu, when you are on the dharma seat you expound dharma profusely; if these talks were compiled into books, wouldn't they fill up a book depository? If this is not endless talking, then what is?"
chapterbody 29: I replied:
chapterbody 30: "Not a single sentence was spoken!"
chapterbody 31: Someone asked me:
chapterbody 32: "Grandmaster Lu has personally written more than two hundred books, an endeavor that has taken forty years of time. This is also equivalent to endless and voluminous words in writing, does that mean this span of time has also been wasted?"
chapterbody 33: I answered:
chapterbody 34: "Not a single word was written!"
chapterbody 35: Someone asked:
chapterbody 36: "Are dialogues between Zen masters also considered as meaningless talk?"
chapterbody 37: I replied:
chapterbody 38: "Teachings."
chapterbody 39: "What do they teach?"
chapterbody 40: "Flashes of lightning and sparks struck from flint, have been through many kalpas."
chapterbody 41: "We don't understand?"
chapterbody 42: I replied:
chapterbody 43: "Don't be a bucket of paint... Quickly start thinking it through!"
chapterbody 44: I would say:
chapterbody 45: Dialogues between Zen masters are absolutely not idle chatter or meaningless talk. Great kind and wise teachers use speech to enlighten people; through the simplest statements, their words carry profound meaning, thunderous and deafening, to teach and guide sentient beings.
chapterbody 47: My dharma talks are "prajna."
chapterbody 48: My writings are the "wisdom of buddhas."

chapterbody 2: "Saying unkind words is 'harsh speech,' and this hateful way of speaking is indeed a source of creating negative karma. Words which hurt others, are a violation of samaya."
chapterbody 4: "Do not reveal the private matters of others, do not hurt others with harsh or abusive words, do not use obscene language, and also do not speak ill of others behind their backs."
chapterbody 6: Obscene language
chapterbody 8: Humiliating or vilifying others
chapterbody 9: Exposing other people's private matters
chapterbody 10: These are all examples of karmic transgressions of "harsh speech" which break the samaya of speech!
chapterbody 12: I remember a famous writer, who was very talented and intelligent, but he committed suicide. Before committing suicide, he left behind a note, on which four words were written: "Human gossip is terrifying!"
chapterbody 13: These four words cause people to tremble with fear.
chapterbody 14: I remember an entrepreneur who was at the peak of his success. Because the media exposed a part of his private life, which was really not such a great failing, he committed suicide by jumping off the Penghu Bridge.
chapterbody 15: When I saw this news, I was greatly saddened.
chapterbody 16: All such events are due to "harsh speech."
chapterbody 18: I realized:
chapterbody 19: Regardless of whether Chinese or foreign media, they all have tabloids. All of them have an attitude of not ceasing until they are able to provoke people with sensational words. They exert their utmost effort, regardless of the truth, and specialize in humiliating, criticizing or defaming others.
chapterbody 20: Many people like to watch these kinds of news, which is also one of the reasons why tabloid journalism is able to continue to exist.
chapterbody 21: Their main subject matter revolves around the exposure of the private lives of famous people.
chapterbody 22: Someone asked me:
chapterbody 23: "Are these kinds of media sinful?"
chapterbody 24: I replied:
chapterbody 25: "If the facts are true, the reports are correct, fair, just and objective, I think that is still acceptable. However, if the facts are biased, the reports are untrue, unjust, unfair and not objective, then I think, that is harsh speech!"
chapterbody 26: Actually, "harsh speech" does carry karmic retribution.
chapterbody 27: Descent into the three lower realms.
chapterbody 28: In future lives, their speech will often anger others.
chapterbody 29: They take pleasure in "harsh speech."
chapterbody 33: I have often said:
chapterbody 34: In this life, I grew amidst hurtful and defamatory speech. If I were to pay attention to these, there would be no end to handling them.
chapterbody 35: Therefore, I ignored all of it.
chapterbody 36: I am aware, since this is the "human world," this is simply how the "human world" is.
chapterbody 37: Even though I clearly know that the defamatory accusations are untrue, however, I also do not wish to engage in debate to clear my name.
chapterbody 38: There was another group that made use of a spiritually disturbed woman, and greatly defamed me, but I did not respond.
chapterbody 39: Regarding news and articles by various media sources, I also did not care. Even if there were a great upheaval and the world was turned upside down, I would not be concerned. Even if disciples saw these reports and decided to turn their backs on me, I would also not care.
chapterbody 40: Why?
chapterbody 41: Because this is the "human world."
chapterbody 45: An awakened person knows that actually all living beings are of one body and all contain buddha-nature.
chapterbody 46: Sentient beings are one's parents or relatives from previous lives. I will not harm sentient beings.
chapterbody 47: Harming others is the same as harming oneself, and hurting others is the same as hurting oneself. Therefore, I vowed, I will never sue people.
chapterbody 48: When others slander me, this is the time they are teaching me to practice the "paramita of patient endurance."
chapterbody 49: Do not commit "harsh speech."
chapterbody 50: Because you are hurting yourself!

chaptername: 024 Craving and Attachment to the Secular World
chapterbody 2: "Spiritual practitioners should avoid clinging to worldly things, because craving for and attachment to the secular world will cause one not to advance, but instead fall. Worldly things include all tangible and intangible objects, such as: fame and status, money and possessions, beauty and sensual pleasures."
chapterbody 4: "A spiritual practitioner's mind of renunciation is very important. If one does not have a transcendental mind of renunciation, cultivation has no benefit. As its name suggests, a mind of renunciation would mean of course that one has to avoid clinging to worldly matters."
chapterbody 6: "Being unwilling to give, being covetous of others' money or possessions, being desirous of beauty or sensual pleasures, covetous of things that do not belong to oneself, craving for and attachment to fine and beautiful objects - these are all instances of clinging to worldly things."
chapterbody 8: I have said:
chapterbody 9: Money
chapterbody 10: Gold
chapterbody 12: Stocks
chapterbody 13: Plush residences
chapterbody 14: Luxury cars
chapterbody 15: These are all fine and beautiful worldly things. It is not wrong to have them, but one should not be too attached to them or crave them excessively. Do not desire for even more or even finer things if one has them already. Accumulation of money and possessions is in fact craving and attachment to the secular world.
chapterbody 16: We are all aware, that when we are born we do not bring anything with us, and when we die we will not be able to take anything with us. Worldly things are "impermanent." Even the rich are only temporary, as are dignitaries, celebrities and beauty; all of these last for only a time.
chapterbody 17: Therefore, White Tara urges us not to cling tightly to money, possessions, reputation, status, and beauty without letting go.
chapterbody 18: Spiritual practitioners know clearly and deeply that everything is "impermanent."
chapterbody 19: They will not crave or cling.
chapterbody 20: They will not be reluctant to give.
chapterbody 21: One should let things come and go naturally. Let what will come, come, and what will go, go. Regarding all fine and beautiful worldly things, let them not dwell in your heart; doing so is the practice of a "quiescent mind," and the practice of a "quiescent mind" is the "practice of prajna."
chapterbody 25: There are three forms of karmic retribution that result from craving and attachment to the secular world:
chapterbody 26: 1. Rebirth in the three lower realms
chapterbody 27: 2. Even if one is reborn as a human being, one will be born in a land subject to famine, drought, and poverty
chapterbody 28: 3. One's conduct will be miserly and petty
chapterbody 29: It is very important for spiritual practitioners to abandon "craving and attachment to the secular world."
chapterbody 33: I remember in the past there was a dharma propagator who competed with me over wealth.
chapterbody 34: I had a single Mercedes-Benz car, he bought three Mercedes-Benz cars.
chapterbody 35: I had one residence, he bought ten houses.
chapterbody 36: Later on, I had Rainbow Villa (which has now become True Buddha Rainbow Temple), and he also built a villa in the central region.
chapterbody 37: He said to everyone he met:
chapterbody 38: "I want to outdo Grandmaster Lu!"
chapterbody 39: I am called Living Buddha Lian-sheng.
chapterbody 40: On his business card, he printed: Living Buddha Lian-X.
chapterbody 41: Later on, he died.
chapterbody 42: I was greatly saddened in my heart.
chapterbody 46: There was another dharma propagator who traded stocks for a living, and he often asked me about the stock market.
chapterbody 47: I said:
chapterbody 48: "Stocks either rise or fall. If the charts don't resemble an 'M,' they would resemble a 'W.' These are all temporary!"
chapterbody 49: I said:
chapterbody 50: "Spiritual practitioners should not play the stock market. You are happy when you make a gain, depressed when you make a loss. Your mind is affected by the ups and downs of the stock market. A practitioner of Buddhism should not behave like this."
chapterbody 52: Later, he abandoned True Buddha School.
chapterbody 53: He was too attached to the secular world!

chapterbody 2: "One cannot hate! Hatred will cause one to descend to the three lower realms for sure, and hatred breaks the samaya vows. Practitioners, be careful, be careful!"
chapterbody 4: "Love and hatred, although opposite, come in fact from the same origin, and they are one and the same. Hatred is enmity towards others. If one has ill will or hostile intent towards others, it already goes against Tao, and consequently one will fall into the three lower realms."
chapterbody 6: "Buddhadharma teaches great loving-kindness to all sentient beings including those whom one has no affinity with. Buddhadharma also teaches that one should have great compassion to others as though they were oneself. One should also have great benevolence and a mind of bodhicitta. Practitioners are able to understand all these dharma teachings, but, why can't they accomplish it?"
chapterbody 8: After hearing White Tara's teaching, I began to sweat.
chapterbody 9: I know that the number of people who can accomplish this are far too few.
chapterbody 10: My view is:
chapterbody 11: The arising of hatred and loathing is due to many factors:
chapterbody 12: 1. Discord between people
chapterbody 13: 2. Jealousy
chapterbody 14: 3. Envy
chapterbody 16: Hatred originates as unkind thoughts, but the actions hatred gives rise to are to be feared. Practitioners have to avoid hatred. Do not commit evil deeds that harm oneself and others due to hatred.
chapterbody 17: We have seen too much of:
chapterbody 18: Discord between people, causing one to kill the other.
chapterbody 19: Out of jealousy, one kills the other.
chapterbody 20: Due to envy, one kills the other.
chapterbody 21: Love turns into hatred, and one kills the other.
chapterbody 22: (These can often be seen in the secular human world. Practitioners have to avoid hatred, and must not even have the thought of hatred, because this breaks the samaya vows.)
chapterbody 24: As Vajrayana practitioners, we have to develop the Four Immeasurable Minds:
chapterbody 25: 1. Infinite loving-kindness - give people happiness
chapterbody 26: 2. Infinite compassion - alleviate others' suffering
chapterbody 27: 3. Infinite joy - offer loving-kindness and compassion joyfully
chapterbody 28: 4. Infinite equanimity - be willing to give even one's life and time
chapterbody 32: My personal belief is:
chapterbody 33: From the perspective that all sentient beings have "buddha-nature," from the perspective that all sentient beings are equal and not different, from the perspective that all sentient beings and oneself are of the same body—
chapterbody 34: Practitioners cannot hate!
chapterbody 35: Hatred will absolutely lead one to fall into the three lower realms! For example:
chapterbody 36: A woman endlessly slandered me, but I cannot hate.
chapterbody 37: A disciple who left, slandered me endlessly, but I cannot hate.
chapterbody 38: Instead, I would vow not to give up on any sentient being, and always give rise to great bodhicitta.
chapterbody 39: Thus so. And so it is. Another example:
chapterbody 40: The affair between former U.S. President Clinton and Monica Lewinsky once made headline news around the world.
chapterbody 41: This is a real example of "love turned to hatred."
chapterbody 42: Hatred is really terrifying.
chapterbody 43: Thus, there are many, many examples of romantic relationships ending violently.
chapterbody 44: When loving each other, the world turns upside down. When loathing each other, the world is similarly thrown into upheaval.
chapterbody 45: Because this is the human realm, and sentient beings are all worldly people.
chapterbody 46: However, the teachings of Buddha are different from those of ordinary people.
chapterbody 47: Practitioners need to accept Buddha's teaching.
chapterbody 48: "One cannot hate!"
chapterbody 49: I would like to tell holy disciples quite frankly, if you have a heart of enmity, then you are not a practitioner of Mahayana, or even a practitioner of Theravada.
chapterbody 50: If someone hates you, you cannot hate him. You must even try to save him instead, only then are you a practitioner!

chapterbody 2: "If one has not yet become enlightened, but claims that one has. This is exaggeration! If one has no supernatural ability, but claims that one has already attained great supernatural ability. This is also exaggeration! When one has neither true enlightenment nor real attainment, and exaggerates oneself, this is the overinflation of one's ego, which becomes false pride and arrogance."
chapterbody 4: "When a person without true enlightenment and attainment talks about Buddhist teachings, this gives rise to prejudiced views. For example, eternalism or nihilism, or other views such as, asceticism is the highest and unsurpassed form of cultivation; the universe is a temporary composite body."
chapterbody 6: "These are all wrong views, which will result in falling into the three lower realms."
chapterbody 8: I said:
chapterbody 9: In this world, very few people indeed truly understand the difference between right views and wrong views.
chapterbody 10: However, enlightened ones know.
chapterbody 11: I would say, for example the view that the universe is a temporary composite body appears to make sense on the surface. However, it is still a wrong view.
chapterbody 12: Others may not know.
chapterbody 13: I know.
chapterbody 16: Most people will praise ascetics. Ascetic cultivators are often recognized by the world as having moral depth and being successful in their cultivation. In fact, this is also a wrong view.
chapterbody 17: Others may not know. I know.
chapterbody 19: Another example:
chapterbody 20: In the past, my elders said to me that there is nothing after one's death. Heaven, hell, karma, retribution and reincarnation are all made up by religious leaders.
chapterbody 21: I heard this and knew right away that this is "nihilism."
chapterbody 22: The belief that there is nothing after death is "nihilism."
chapterbody 24: Yet another example:
chapterbody 25: Someone said to me that he does not believe in anything other than "money." Only "money" is real.
chapterbody 26: Luxurious houses, prestigious cars, beautiful women, diamonds, jewels, fame and profit, status... are all attainable.
chapterbody 27: Everything is real.
chapterbody 28: We should enjoy life in this real world.
chapterbody 29: I heard this and knew right away that this is "eternalism."
chapterbody 30: Being attached to the material world and not letting go is "eternalism."
chapterbody 31: Both "nihilism" and "eternalism" are wrong views.
chapterbody 35: I, Living Buddha Lian-sheng, Sheng-Yen Lu, can criticize all cultivation practitioners. Why? Because I am enlightened.
chapterbody 36: In this world, if you have not reached enlightenment and you criticize others, it is then "slander," and it is then pride and prejudice. The evil guiding the evil, the lost guiding the lost, and the blind guiding the blind, and all will go into the fiery pit.
chapterbody 37: However, if you are truly enlightened, you can criticize all cultivation practitioners. This is a way to guide them to attain unsurpassed and perfect enlightenment. It is a kind of lion's roar, a voice of justice, to awaken the masses so that they may attain "right views."
chapterbody 38: In fact:
chapterbody 39: "Eternalism" and "nihilism" will lead to the three lower realms.
chapterbody 40: "Asceticism" is a wrong view.
chapterbody 41: "The universe is a temporary composite body" is a wrong view.…
chapterbody 42: In this world, there are many people who consider themselves learned, and they created a lot of ideas and theories.
chapterbody 43: These ideas and theories are based on "wrong views."
chapterbody 44: Now only those who are enlightened, are truly able to see through these ideas or theories using the "wisdom of buddhas."
chapterbody 45: If one has any doubts?
chapterbody 46: It seems, one can only ask "me!"

chapterbody 2: "Lian-sheng, Grandmaster Lu, you must explain the most serious samaya precepts. A cultivating practitioner who breaks any of these precepts will definitely fall into the three lower realms of hell, hungry ghosts, or animals. The extent of punishment would be ceaseless in both time and space. The agony is unimaginable - a billion times more painful than what one can experience in this world."
chapterbody 4: White Tara said:
chapterbody 5: "The reason is because the people involved and circumstances are different, consequently resulting in the differences in severity. Grandmaster Lu, you must explain it."
chapterbody 8: The most severe transgression of killing -- is when you terminate the life of a cultivator who has unsurpassed and perfect enlightenment. As the life of this cultivator is invaluable, if you kill such a practitioner, it is equivalent to killing the wisdom-lives of hundreds of thousands of people.
chapterbody 9: (Killing a buddha or bodhisattva results in the most severe karmic retribution.)
chapterbody 11: The most serious transgression of theft -- is stealing from a temple, a cultivation chapter, or a cultivation association. Transferring the ownership of a temple, a cultivation chapter, or a cultivation association to one's own name, embezzling money and property that does not belong to you.
chapterbody 12: (Or fraudulent fundraising under the pretext of building temples, cultivation chapters or associations, and then subsequently misappropriating the funds for one's personal gain and use; these are the most serious forms of theft.)
chapterbody 14: The most severe transgression of sexual misconduct -- is when one seduces a monk or nun to violate their vows of celibacy. The resulting negative karma from this is much heavier than for people in general. If you force a bodhisattva to perform a sexual act with you, this also carries extremely heavy negative karma.
chapterbody 15: (In other words, the negative karma is doubled when one entices a cultivating practitioner to commit sexually immoral acts.)
chapterbody 17: The most serious transgression of dishonest speech -- is when one deceives one's Root Guru, deceives one's teacher, and deceives the holy sangha. Committing an act of fraud against one's Root Guru for fame and status, fortune, and so on, in whatever way, will certainly result in one descending into Vajra Hell.
chapterbody 18: (It is useless and fruitless for one who deceives his/her Root Guru to carry out cultivation. This is due to the loss of lineage and blessing from one's Root Guru.)
chapterbody 20: The most serious transgression of double-tongue -- is when one sows discord in the monastic community, and causes a schism within the Sangha. The Buddhist Sangha is the main group of cultivating practitioners, if you contrive to use divisive speech to sow discord and the Sangha is split asunder, the severe negative karma from this is the worst.
chapterbody 21: (The foremost example of this is Devadatta who betrayed the Buddha and caused a schism within the Sangha.)
chapterbody 23: The most serious transgression of harsh speech -- is when one insults one's Root Guru with the most vicious and abusive words, or the filthiest speech. When one utters slander from only hearsay, from false rumors in circulation, or fictitious fabrications that one did not personally witness; the moment harsh speech arises, one has committed a grave sin. It is also a grave transgression when one uses abusive speech on a genuine cultivating practitioner.
chapterbody 25: The most severe transgression of loose speech -- is when you disrupt other people's cultivation through meaningless idle gossip, or when you perturb the practice of a sincere cultivating practitioner or arhat through telling lewd jokes.
chapterbody 27: The most severe form of greed -- is coveting money belonging to the Three Jewels (the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha).
chapterbody 28: (For example, if one takes the monetary offerings intended for one's Root Guru, privately taking possession of it and flees. Or a treasurer of a temple, cultivation chapter, or association absconding with funds belonging to the cultivation venue. Or if one receives kickbacks from selling buddhist implements.)
chapterbody 30: The worst evil thoughts -- are not wanting to leave the cycle of reincarnation, not wanting to liberate oneself from worries and afflictions, not wanting to attain self-mastery over life and death. Instead, your mind is filled with thoughts of worldly wrongdoings, especially the "Five Deadly Sins" or "Five Heinous Crimes."
chapterbody 32: The most severe wrong views -- are slander against buddha-nature, slander against the wisdom of Tathagatas, slander against the Three Dharma Seals, slander against the teaching of One Ultimate Reality, slander against the Buddha, Dharma, Sangha, and slander against your Root Guru.
chapterbody 34: White Tara said:
chapterbody 35: "Teachings regarding the precepts are of paramount importance, and they should be taught all the time."
chapterbody 36: I asked:
chapterbody 37: "In the past, I have already explained them in my books. Do I still need to repeat them?"
chapterbody 38: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 39: "Precepts are the foundation of all dharma. It is necessary to repeat them time and again. If one breaks one's samaya precepts, not only will one be unable to reach accomplishment, but one will also fall into the three lower realms."

chapterbody: uddhism originally referred to the teachings of the Buddha. Buddhism spread from India to Southeast Asia, China, Korea, Japan... and many other places.
chapterbody 2: In early Buddhism, because of many various situations that arose, the Buddha then promulgated precepts and a regulatory system.
chapterbody 3: What is in accord with Dharma? What is not in accord with Dharma?
chapterbody 4: In early Buddhism, bhikkhus and bhikkhunis who renounced worldly life, and upasakas and upasikas were known as the fourfold assembly.
chapterbody 6: Bhikkhunis had second place.
chapterbody 7: Upasakas (laymen devotees) were next in standing.
chapterbody 8: Upasikas (laywomen devotees) had the lowest standing.
chapterbody 9: There were many regulations, which are rather complicated to discuss.
chapterbody 11: However, in today's era, equality has now been established between men and women. As the situation has changed somewhat, I specially consulted White Tara regarding Buddhist regulations, and requested teachings from her.
chapterbody 12: I asked:
chapterbody 13: "In today's era, do we have to comply with each and every rule of early Buddhism?"
chapterbody 14: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 15: "It would be best to comply with Buddha's regulations. A few minor adjustments are still alright, but, it would be inappropriate to deviate from them."
chapterbody 16: I asked:
chapterbody 17: "In the early days, True Buddha School had masters who were not monks or nuns. Is it appropriate for bhikkhus and bhikkhunis to prostrate themselves at the feet of a lay master?"
chapterbody 18: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 19: "It is inappropriate. It would be best for lay masters to renunciate in a timely manner, to transform what is inappropriate into what is appropriate. The occurrence of laity being above and renunciants below, secular folk above and bhikkhus below, prostrating to those above, is an aberrant phenomenon of the dharma ending age. According to Buddhist sutras, these are manifestations of mara and do not comply with Buddhist regulations."
chapterbody 20: I asked:
chapterbody 21: "In ancient times, there was a great disparity between bhikkhus and bhikkhunis. In today's age, there is equality between men and women; do bhikkhus and bhikkhunis have equal standing now?"
chapterbody 22: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 23: "When they have become buddhas and bodhisattvas, they are of course equal. However, in the human world, there are still regulations. Although there are people who advocate reforms, however generally speaking, various Buddhist schools still comply with Buddhist regulations."
chapterbody 24: I asked:
chapterbody 25: "Is it appropriate for a lay master to take the seat in the center, while bhikkhus and bhikkhunis sit in two rows at the sides?"
chapterbody 26: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 27: "This is not acceptable. It does not comply with Buddhist regulations at all. It would be best if the lay master sits in the center, and bhikkhus and bhikkhunis sit at the back of the master. This demonstrates respect to those who have renounced worldly life, and is also an expedient means."
chapterbody 28: I said:
chapterbody 29: "If a layperson sits at the center while wearing the five-buddhas- crown, and bhikkhus and bhikkhunis prostrate towards the center, is it okay?"
chapterbody 30: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 31: "Prostrating to the Five Buddhas is permissible; prostrating to the person is not permissible. This is also an expedient means."
chapterbody 32: I asked:
chapterbody 33: "What should we do?" White Tara replied:
chapterbody 34: "It would be best for the master to renounce secular life. By doing so, all problems would be readily solved."
chapterbody 35: "What if there are those who do not want to renounce secular life?" I said.
chapterbody 36: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 37: "Three important things in Esoteric Buddhism: bodhicitta, a transcendental mind of renunciation, the Middle Path and Right Views. If one does not have a mind of renouncing the secular world, how is such a person a practitioner?"
chapterbody 38: I said:
chapterbody 39: "I once said, that laypeople and renunciants are equal, is this statement wrong?"
chapterbody 40: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 41: "It is not wrong. At least with regard to cultivation and realization, they are equal and there is no discrimination; they are both capable of enlightenment and accomplishment. However, with regard to Buddhist regulations, the sangha maintains and teaches Buddhadharma, while laypeople support Buddhadharma."

chapterbody: oday, True Buddha School disciples can be found all over the world. They are of different races, speaking various languages, and have different habits and dispositions. While dharma propagators work hard and travel around the world to spread Buddhist teachings, however, there are also "weeds" hidden amongst them.
chapterbody 2: Many disciples of True Buddha School have asked me:
chapterbody 3: "What criteria constitute a real practitioner? Across all levels of disciples such as masters, senior reverends and senior dharma instructors, reverends, dharma instructors, dharma assistants, chapter chairpersons, and disciples of True Buddha School, we would like to ask Grandmaster Lu, what is a model True Buddha School practitioner?"
chapterbody 5: I passed this question on to White Tara.
chapterbody 6: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 7: To be a model True Buddha School practitioner, there are ten minimum criteria:
chapterbody 9: 1. He/she never kills. Not only does he/she not kill, he/she releases living things, and saves lives.
chapterbody 10: He/she could be a vegetarian.
chapterbody 11: If he/she is not a vegetarian, he/she should know the dharma methods of "making offerings," "deliverance" and "purification."
chapterbody 13: 2. He/she is not stingy and likes to engage in kind deeds and charity, donating money as well as energy and time, giving rise to many kind affinities and relationships all around, and is a great Samaritan who gladly engages in philanthropy.
chapterbody 14: If he/she is poor, he/she enjoys doing volunteer work.
chapterbody 17: 3. He/she lives a pure and honest life. No smoking, no drinking, no card playing or mahjong, no gambling, no sexual misconduct, no fighting.
chapterbody 18: He/she does not indulge in comfort and pleasures.
chapterbody 19: Does not hate hard work.
chapterbody 20: Is optimistic and diligent.
chapterbody 22: 4. He/she is an honest person, and is not hypocritical towards his/ her Root Guru. He/she is respectful to the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha, values and honors Buddhadharma, and respects all practitioners.
chapterbody 23: He/she does not lie.
chapterbody 24: Does not utter abusive speech.
chapterbody 25: Does not cause discord amongst others.
chapterbody 26: Does not speak meaningless words.
chapterbody 27: Being honest and sincere is not easy.
chapterbody 29: 5. He/she is intelligent and also wise, able to discern the cause and effect of good and evil, and see clearly the right and wrong doings in the human world. He/she has the ability to reconcile differences between others.

chapterbody 2: "What is the significance of the dharma of 'selflessness'?"
chapterbody 4: "The cultivation of one's buddha-nature."
chapterbody 6: "How do we practice this?"
chapterbody 8: "Gradually remove attachment to the distinction of dualities."
chapterbody 9: I asked:
chapterbody 10: "How do we put this in actual practice?"
chapterbody 12: "Stay away from all affectations."
chapterbody 13: I asked:
chapterbody 14: "Is it about being natural?"
chapterbody 16: "With regard to worldly gain and loss, fame and insignificance, praise and blame or ridicule, joy and sorrow, one sees and understands clearly, yet one's mind is unfettered. As a result, one's mind is at rest. Without thought, without thought, without attachment, without attachment."
chapterbody 17: I asked:
chapterbody 18: "Is this the Emptiness of the Three Wheels?"
chapterbody 19: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 20: "It is, it is."
chapterbody 24: White Tara said:
chapterbody 25: "Many practitioners have selfish thoughts. There are also some selfish people who follow selfish practitioners. These practitioners are in fact foolish and ignorant, and their followers are even more ignorant. As long as one has selfish thoughts, or is still attached to the distinction of dualities, one will not be able to realize the selfless buddha-nature. Not only that, one will also fall into the three lower realms!"
chapterbody 26: White Tara said:
chapterbody 27: "Selfish practitioners are people who are 'self '-centered, thus such individuals will naturally have a distinction between enemy and friend, self and others. In this way, these practitioners end up going in the opposite direction of bodhicitta, and as a result, these practitioners sink deeper and deeper into quagmire, beyond help or remedy, and fall into the three lower realms. The practitioners themselves and all their followers will all enter into the fiery pit."
chapterbody 28: "Why would they all fall into the fiery pit?" I asked.
chapterbody 29: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 30: "One who is attached to dualities, recognizes those who bring benefit as friends, and those who are harmful as enemies. In this way, duality creates attachment and aversion. From these two, one creates negative karma, which leads one to reincarnate and descend to the three lower realms."
chapterbody 31: White Tara said:
chapterbody 32: "Remove human selfishness! This self is mara!"
chapterbody 34: White Tara's teachings are:
chapterbody 35: One practices selflessness, and teaches everyone to be selfless. Follow selfless people.
chapterbody 36: Follow a selfless Root Guru and selfless wise and virtuous friends. Do not follow selfish practitioners, or those concerned only with their own self-interests, because they have not realized selflessness.
chapterbody 37: Abandon distinctions, such as those of nations, families, clans, territories, rank and status, enemies, friends, and so on.
chapterbody 38: Do not crave fame. Do not crave profit.
chapterbody 39: Do not be attached to one's own flesh body.
chapterbody 40: Do not be attached to one's own family or clan.
chapterbody 41: Become familiar with selflessness.
chapterbody 42: Become familiar with buddha-nature.
chapterbody 43: Know that all names and appearances have no real nature.
chapterbody 44: Know that all worldly beings and myriad phenomena have no real nature.
chapterbody 45: Know that things seem to exist, but in fact they are like dreams, illusions, bubbles, and shadows.
chapterbody 46: Nothing to obtain.
chapterbody 47: Nothing to have.
chapterbody 48: Nowhere to reside.

Therefore, he should comprehend samsara and nirvana, affliction and bodhi, reality and expedient methods, and see the original dharma body.

chapterbody 36: If there is no such transmission of this pivotal dharma teaching, it would not be possible to cut off afflictions, nor to attain self-mastery over life and death, nor become liberated, and realize enlightenment.
