chapterbody: randmaster Lu descended into the world in an ordinary family on June 27, 1945, which was also the time when the U.S. air force was bombing the Shuishang Airport at Chiayi in Taiwan.
chapterbody 2: The whole family moved to the banks of the Niuchou Creek, where I was born inside a hen house. That was the year of the Rooster. When I was born, my whole body was wrapped with what looked like "white gauze."
chapterbody 3: My mother said that I was born prematurely.
chapterbody 4: My father thought that this baby was born in an odd fashion.
chapterbody 5: I was not taken care of seriously and was treated like an alien.
chapterbody 6: Thus, there was a period of time when I was sent to be raised by my aunt. My aunt's name is Huang Xue-hua.
chapterbody 7: As a newborn baby, I did not understand anything. Later on, I understood. There were several indications contained within my birth:
chapterbody 8: First, the white gauze - a white heavenly garment
chapterbody 9: Second, the background and origin - a bodhisattva
chapterbody 10: Third, my birth - a rare event
chapterbody 11: (An extraordinary whole drama is hidden inside)
chapterbody 16: "Why is my birth a whole drama?"
chapterbody 17: White Tara said:
chapterbody 18: "You have an extraordinary background."
chapterbody 19: I asked:
chapterbody 20: "How is it extraordinary?"
chapterbody 21: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 22: "You directly entered into the lotus-embryo which was not contaminated by your father's sperm and mother's blood, and you were born prematurely. Your diligent practice of Taoism, Exoteric Buddhism, and Vajrayana Buddhism finally led to your realization of enlightenment and seeing buddha-nature. Delivering sentient beings extensively, you benefit people and divine beings."
chapterbody 23: I asked:
chapterbody 24: "What is my origin?"
chapterbody 25: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 26: "Padmakumara descended into this world, Amitabha Buddha is your original body, Vairocana Buddha is your source."
chapterbody 27: I asked:
chapterbody 28: "What about White Tara's origin?"
chapterbody 29: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 30: "Maha Prajnaparamita Buddha-Mother is my origin."
chapterbody 34: I asked:
chapterbody 35: "How will this whole drama unfold?"
chapterbody 36: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 37: "You realized enlightenment and saw buddha-nature, breaking away from the cycle of transmigration, transmitting the highest and unsurpassed dharma practices, thoroughly understanding all dharma teachings and eighty-four thousand entryways to dharma, and founded a school to benefit all sentient beings."
chapterbody 38: I asked:
chapterbody 39: "How do I teach sentient beings?"
chapterbody 40: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 41: "Grandmaster Lu generates all merits and skillfully utilizes various methods to bring sentient beings' habits under control."
chapterbody 42: I asked:
chapterbody 43: "What about disciples who still do not abide by the precepts, and are still unrestrained?"
chapterbody 44: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 45: "Having complete pervasive knowledge, clear comprehension of the causes of birth and death, a mind free from discrimination, and benefitting all sentient beings equally, even if there are disciples who are still unrestrained, Grandmaster Lu would understand the cause and effect and the origin."
chapterbody 46: I asked:
chapterbody 47: "How do I guide disciples who do not follow the precepts?"
chapterbody 48: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 49: "With confidence, great compassion, and a mind of altruism, intelligent and wise Grandmaster Lu, you must know that no one is left behind."
chapterbody 50: I nodded.
chapterbody 52: I understand, that as a Root Guru, I am the center of all buddhas and bodhisattvas' hearts. Whoever abandons the center of all buddhas and bodhisattvas' hearts, is not following the precepts. This is something I deeply understand.
chapterbody 53: It is too bad that sentient beings only have a hazy notion and do not fully understand, and they make mistakes time and again. After all, this is the human world!

chapterbody 2: "A sectarian viewpoint is a great fault among practitioners today, and most practitioners make this mistake."
chapterbody 4: "When practitioners make this mistake, they then create negative karma of speech, body, and mind without realizing it. Even after falling into the three lower realms, they still do not know what exactly they did wrong."
chapterbody 5: After listening to the words of White Tara, I suddenly saw the light.
chapterbody 6: I would say:
chapterbody 7: Practitioners cannot have a sectarian perspective, instead, they should be like Sudhana who visited fifty-three wise teachers and understood thoroughly all dharma teachings.
chapterbody 8: All practitioners know the story of Sudhana's visiting fifty-three wise teachers, but, not one practitioner can actually do it.
chapterbody 9: I, Grandmaster Lu, have roamed the twenty-eight heavens, have traveled to the four holy realms, and have even journeyed down to the three lower realms. Seeing the profound depths of the earth, the vast expanse of the earth, I am not one of the great or small masters with little eyes and little noses, who each reside on their own hills. Hence, I have no sectarian viewpoint.
chapterbody 10: Because I am not limited by a sectarian viewpoint, I am able to have right and pervasive knowledge, fully understand that all methods have no real nature, and know that all dharma methods are expedient means. Hence, I am able to be fearless regarding everything, and arrive at the state of "indifference."
chapterbody 12: Because my heart is wide and open, hence each sect is not a sect, each method is not a method. I clearly and thoroughly comprehend the darkness of all ignorance. I am like a lamp, and the moment I enter into a dark chamber, all things are lit up and can be seen.
chapterbody 13: (When Avalokitesvara Bodhisattva was practicing the profound Prajnaparamita, he illuminated the Five Skandhas and saw that they were all empty.)
chapterbody 16: Free oneself through all methods, free oneself from all methods, can you do this?
chapterbody 17: One method connects to all methods, can you do this?
chapterbody 18: Progress gradually step by step, can you do this?
chapterbody 19: Spontaneous enlightenment, can you do this?
chapterbody 20: Thus, you would not violate samaya, can you do this?
chapterbody 21: (Neither produced nor destroyed, neither defiled nor pure, neither increasing nor diminishing.)
chapterbody 22: Grandmaster Lu learned all dharma teachings, and diligently practiced all appearances of dharma, thus I became freed from all sectarianism.
chapterbody 23: What is a "true buddha"?
chapterbody 24: A magnitude to contain all, and being free from everything, is a "true buddha."
chapterbody 25: Comprehend thoroughly the roots of all dharma teachings, and understand all Buddhist sects.
chapterbody 26: The ultimate is merging into one, all dharma teachings are of one-taste, and one-taste will also return to zero.
chapterbody 27: When prajna ascends and reaches the peak of perfection, and one understands thoroughly the true significance of all teachings, this is: "discovering the true mind and seeing buddha-nature."
chapterbody 31: White Tara said:
chapterbody 32: Samaya related to a sectarian viewpoint include:
chapterbody 33: Desiring all dharma methods, but, unable to thoroughly understand and accomplish any of them.
chapterbody 34: Unable to attain yogic union, unable to return to zero. Not being able to see the true nature of the dharma realm.
chapterbody 35: Practicing blindly and foolishly, without the lineage of pivotal key teachings from one's Root Guru.
chapterbody 36: Following an ignorant master.
chapterbody 37: Not abiding by samaya vows.
chapterbody 38: In absolute ignorance whether one's conduct is in accord with dharma or not.
chapterbody 39: Not knowing that Buddhadharma is only of one-taste, and not knowing the true insights of Buddhadharma.
chapterbody 40: What are the fundamentals of Buddhism? No clue.
chapterbody 41: Blindly echoing the views of others, and when standing on one hilltop, verbally attacking all other hilltops.
chapterbody 42: Not seeing and realizing dharma nature.
chapterbody 43: Not yet enlightened, but have already established a large religious domain. With the blind leading the blind, all will fall into the fiery pit.
chapterbody 44: My opinion is:
chapterbody 45: So-called great masters, if they have not yet attained enlightenment, will have sectarian viewpoints, and discriminate against other sects. Attacking each other with wrong views, in this way they would certainly break the samaya vows.

chaptername: 034 On Retreating from the Mind of Tao
chapterbody 2: "If precepts are too strict, or we rigorously enforce Buddhist regulations, will it cause cultivation practitioners to retreat from the mind of Tao?"
chapterbody 4: "The mind of Tao is built on faith. People who believe in Buddhism trust deeply that Buddhadharma can save sentient beings from samsara. Those who continually rely upon this faith, and who have confidence, will definitely abide by the precepts, and respect Buddhist regulations."
chapterbody 6: "Some people say, they have retreated from the path of Tao."
chapterbody 8: "If a person has a mind of Tao, he or she will never retreat."
chapterbody 9: I asked:
chapterbody 10: "If he/she has retreated?"
chapterbody 12: "He/she never had a mind of Tao."
chapterbody 13: I pondered this for a long time. A mind of Tao, a mind of Tao, retreating from the path of Tao, retreating from the mind of Tao...
chapterbody 14: Consequently:
chapterbody 15: Someone said: "I want to retreat from the path of Tao!"
chapterbody 16: I answered: "One who has a mind of Tao, will never retreat. You never had the mind of Tao after all."
chapterbody 17: Someone said:
chapterbody 18: "I want to resume secular life!"
chapterbody 19: I replied:
chapterbody 20: "When have you ever lived the holy life? Being secular from the beginning, do you still need to resume secular life?"
chapterbody 21: (Please think very carefully about this issue.)
chapterbody 25: White Tara said:
chapterbody 26: "The mind of Tao is like the great sun, forever present. Suspended in the empty space, it is always there, and it never retreats or disappears. So-called retreat is one's own ignorance, cravings, hatred, and delusions, covering up the great sun. The great sun will never be lost; it is oneself who becomes lost, and not the mind of Tao."
chapterbody 27: I asked White Tara:
chapterbody 28: "I have always advocated freedom and democracy. Would it be too severe to ask everyone to abide by precepts and follow Buddhist regulations?"
chapterbody 29: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 30: "So-called precepts are for the cessation of immorality. So-called regulations are guiding principles. These are to teach people to avoid and become free from faults, prejudices, hatred, discrimination, confusion, and lack of propriety."
chapterbody 31: White Tara continued:
chapterbody 32: "Cultivators who strictly follow Buddhist regulations are like the dawn sky, totally clear and lucid. They are as transparent as crystal orbs. As the adage goes, a nation has national laws, a family has family rules, a sect has its regulations, and a temple has its rules. Total freedom and democracy would mean utter chaos."
chapterbody 33: I asked:
chapterbody 34: "What does True Buddha School lack?"
chapterbody 35: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 36: "Management."
chapterbody 37: "What is management?" I asked.
chapterbody 38: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 39: "Precepts and regulations."
chapterbody 40: I asked:
chapterbody 41: "What do our dharma propagators lack?"
chapterbody 42: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 43: "Some of them only seem to focus on money and material matters only. They expand their outreach, for the sake of making money. The dissemination of Buddhadharma is merely a pretext for them, and Buddhadharma and cultivation are not their core. This portion of people are aware that wealth cannot be carried away after death, but they continue ceaselessly in their delusion. Grandmaster Lu, how can you ensure that they do not fall into the three lower realms?"
chapterbody 44: I was stunned.
chapterbody 45: "They already have supernatural powers!" I said.
chapterbody 46: White Tara responded:
chapterbody 47: "What they have are money-snatching powers, not supernatural powers."

chapterbody 2: "What they have are money-snatching powers and not supernatural powers!"
chapterbody 3: This statement led me to recall some fortune-tellers on the street, who specialize in telling people the "karma linking their three lives of the past, present, and future." (Knowing the karma of a person's three lives is a "supernatural power.")
chapterbody 4: The fortune-teller would spin out tales about believers' karma in their past, present, and future lives. Then he would strike fear into your heart, by saying that some unfortunate event would happen.
chapterbody 5: After hearing these predictions your heart is filled with anxiety. The fortune-teller then says that these unfortunate mishaps can be dispelled or averted. However, a large sum of money is needed to ward off these calamities.
chapterbody 6: Consequently, you give a large sum of money. If all is well, the fortune-teller has successfully averted the disaster, and everyone is happy. If something bad actually happens, then the fortune-teller was also not wrong in his/her prediction, just that the sum of money to avert the disaster was insufficient, and more money is needed to "replenish one's fortune."
chapterbody 7: Can one believe in these accounts of karma linking past, present, and future lives? Amongst these, I feel that fake ones are in the majority. Many fortune-tellers who claim to have clairvoyance are swindlers with deceitfully persuasive tongues. Their "clairvoyance" is all a sham.
chapterbody 12: There are many false claims of supernatural ability. For example, those without actual cultivation and mastery, who self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial.
chapterbody 13: If they are selfish and only intend to benefit themselves, they ask for money whether their mouths are open or closed, keep requesting money day in and day out, and self-proclaim to have clairvoyance, then their clairvoyance is definitely superficial.
chapterbody 14: If they do not abide by samaya, go against their Root Guru's wishes, not do what their Root Guru wants them to do, but instead, do the things that their Root Guru does not want them to do, and self-proclaim to have great clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial.
chapterbody 15: If they do not abide by the precepts, and violate the precepts pertaining to greed, anger, ignorance, doubt, arrogance, killing, stealing, lust, false speech, drinking of alcohol, and self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial.
chapterbody 16: If they do not have any genuine mastery of deep meditation and stability at all, and are merely depending on communication with ghosts, sprites, low-level spiritual entities, animal spirits, or minor deities, and self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is extremely superficial.
chapterbody 17: Clairvoyance that did not arise from the cultivation of Buddhadharma, but derived from malevolent spirits. If they do not have any true knowledge of dharma or understanding of prajna at all, and self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial.
chapterbody 18: If they do not have bodhicitta, and do not know compassion for sentient beings, neither engaging at all in any deeds that benefit others, nor taking delight in charity whenever the situation arises, but are avidly zealous about the management of business enterprises, and self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial.
chapterbody 19: If they do not have a transcendental mind of renunciation, and all their actions are worldly and secular - trading in stocks, opening retail stores, buying and selling, dealing in currency exchange, gambling, frequenting pornographic establishments, dancing, singing, indulging in pleasures, competing for fame, contending for profit and gain, vying for territory... and they self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial.
chapterbody 20: If they lack right views, are deeply entrenched in habitual tendencies arising from ignorance, have many wrong views, and are enveloped by wickedness; if what they spread is not Buddhadharma, but instead they encourage people to only aspire for rebirth in the heavens, and self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial. If their speech and actions are not in accord with Dharma, and they self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial.
chapterbody 21: If they only know the meditation states of the Four Dhyana Heavens, from the First Dhyana Heaven to the Formless Heaven, are unaware that there are deeper and more profound states of meditation, and self-proclaim to have clairvoyance - this kind of clairvoyance is superficial.
chapterbody 22: False clairvoyance
chapterbody 23: Clairvoyance from ghosts and spirits
chapterbody 24: Minor clairvoyance
chapterbody 25: Clairvoyance from malevolent spirits
chapterbody 26: Clairvoyance from deities
chapterbody 27: Clairvoyance from the Four Dhyana meditation states
chapterbody 28: White Tara said:
chapterbody 29: "These individuals who self-proclaim to have supernatural ability are still within the confines of superficial clairvoyance, not to mention others."

chapterbody 2: "Only people who have severed and extinguished all their worries can qualify as saints or sages, great kind and wise friends, bodhisattvas, and buddhas."
chapterbody 4: "In this world, who is worry-free?"
chapterbody 6: "Grandmaster Lu!"
chapterbody 7: I laughed out loud, "Ha, ha, ha."
chapterbody 8: Then I continued:
chapterbody 9: "In fact, I have a great many troubles! Why would you, White Tara, say that I have no worries?"
chapterbody 10: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 11: "You do not dwell on your troubles or let them bother you, hence, you have severed and extinguished all worries."
chapterbody 12: I said:
chapterbody 13: "This statement is spot on."
chapterbody 17: White Tara said:
chapterbody 18: If a Buddhist cultivator does not cultivate his mind to free himself of worries and afflictions, then even a thousand empowerments, or ten thousand empowerments would be of no use.
chapterbody 19: If a Buddhist cultivator has already reached enlightenment, but still has worries, then he is only enlightened in theory. In practice, he has not actualized enlightenment, so this still does not count as enlightenment.
chapterbody 20: If a Buddhist cultivator has faith in Dharma, but worries accumulate and increase as he cultivates, then there must be a mistake in his cultivation. For if cultivation cannot remedy worries, then this sort of cultivation is really of no benefit.
chapterbody 21: If a Buddhist cultivator practices extremely diligently, but the affliction of worries remains. He must seek and ask his Root Guru (a great kind and wise friend), where exactly did he go wrong in his cultivation?
chapterbody 22: If in the beginning one cultivates very diligently, but later on becomes weary of cultivation, slackens in practice, and feels no sense of accomplishment, then quickly go and ask one's Root Guru for advice about this problem!
chapterbody 23: How does one cultivate? With cultivation, some people's hearts become more confused and distracted, never at rest or tranquil, perpetually uneasy.
chapterbody 25: Venerable Master Sheng Yen has said: "A heart at ease is peace!"
chapterbody 26: This statement is very well said.
chapterbody 27: However, in this world, how many people are truly able to arrive at the state of "a heart at ease"?
chapterbody 28: I would like to ask Venerable Master Sheng Yen:
chapterbody 29: "How does one have a heart at ease?"
chapterbody 30: I would tell everyone quite frankly, in this world, only one person has arrived at the state of a heart at ease, and this person is Living Buddha Lian-sheng, Sheng-Yen Lu.
chapterbody 31: Ha ha ha!
chapterbody 33: White Tara said:
chapterbody 34: "One must cultivate precepts, meditation, and wisdom. One must be able to discover the essence of one's mind and realize one's buddha-nature. One has to actualize the dharma practice of a quiescent mind, understand the meaning of the secular world, and understand the absolute truths of Buddhadharma."
chapterbody 35: If meditation is not able to sever and extinguish worries, if abiding by precepts cannot stop worries, if wisdom is also unable to bring an end to worries and troubles, not even the slightest bit, then the only recourse is to seek and ask advice from Root Guru Sheng-Yen Lu.
chapterbody 36: What should one do when facing suffering and adverse conditions?
chapterbody 37: Grandmaster Lu says:
chapterbody 38: Transform.
chapterbody 39: If one is unable to transform? Then what?
chapterbody 40: Detach.
chapterbody 41: If one is unable to detach from the problem? Then what?
chapterbody 42: Emptiness.
chapterbody 43: If one is unable to reach emptiness? Then what?
chapterbody 44: Then, I will tell you the true nature of worries, the true nature of humans, the true nature of the world, the true nature of sentient beings...
chapterbody 45: If you understand and realize this, then you are "Shakyamuni Buddha!"

chapterbody 2: "Cultivators often speak of attainment, and some people even equate attainment with supernatural power. Actually, this is a very laughable matter, because attainment and supernatural powers are completely unrelated."
chapterbody 4: "Then what is attainment?" I asked.
chapterbody 6: A cultivator is able to face and endure all kinds of suffering and hardships, and without feelings of suffering. This is the attainment of the realization of impermanence.
chapterbody 8: When worries and afflictions do not arise in the mind of a cultivator, or even if worries arise, the cultivator is able to cease or subdue it immediately. This is the attainment of defeating the mara of worries and troubles.
chapterbody 10: In all situations, whether favorable or adverse, a cultivator is always able to quiet his mind, to be completely without waves.
chapterbody 11: This is the attainment of meditative stability.
chapterbody 13: A cultivator never tires of dharma practice, but cultivates diligently and persistently without slackening.
chapterbody 14: This is the attainment of the spontaneous accomplishment of the mind and qi.
chapterbody 16: A cultivator regards his Root Guru as Buddha, and perceives his Root Guru's merits to be of Buddha, and his Root Guru's shortcomings as teachings.
chapterbody 17: This is the attainment of equanimity.
chapterbody 19: When erroneous or discursive thoughts do not arise in a cultivator
chapterbody 20: (The attainment of stability)
chapterbody 21: When a cultivator dwells continuously in the lucid clarity of enlightenment
chapterbody 22: (The attainment of wisdom)
chapterbody 23: When a cultivator is immune to the Five Poisons
chapterbody 24: (The attainment of precepts)
chapterbody 25: White Tara said:
chapterbody 26: "True attainment refers to these. Only these are attainment. As for divine sight, divine hearing, knowledge of the minds of others, knowledge of previous lifetimes, unimpeded bodily action, and the cessation of outflows - these are supernatural powers, but only the power of the cessation of outflows can be counted as the attainment of the complete extinction of afflictions."
chapterbody 30: White Tara continued:
chapterbody 31: Aside from these, there are some other small attainments, which will also be specially mentioned here:
chapterbody 32: Having less attachments.
chapterbody 33: Less worries and troubles, and a bit more understanding and clarity.
chapterbody 34: Not as selfish as before.
chapterbody 35: Less desire for fame and gain, letting things come and go naturally.
chapterbody 36: No longer envious of those more successful than oneself.
chapterbody 37: Departed from craving, seeking and gaining.
chapterbody 38: Abiding in the lucid clarity of meditative cultivation even when not meditating.
chapterbody 39: Whenever any thought arises, a cultivator is able to banish thoughts instantly without letting them dwell in his/her mind; this is the dharma practice of a quiescent mind.
chapterbody 40: Being accepting of both nirvana and reincarnation; this is the attainment of a mind of equanimity.
chapterbody 41: No longer seeking special relationships to climb the social ladder.
chapterbody 42: Abiding peacefully in all matters.
chapterbody 46: White Tara said:
chapterbody 47: There are some cultivators who self-proclaim that they have already achieved attainment, and their attainment is extremely great, so much so that they have ascended beyond nine levels of heavens.
chapterbody 48: If these cultivators:
chapterbody 49: Crave fame and status
chapterbody 50: Contend for profit and gain
chapterbody 51: Exaggerate false claims of supernatural power
chapterbody 53: Mislead other cultivators (not in accord with Dharma)

chaptername: 038 Encountering a Rare and Precious Master
chapterbody 2: "In this human world, it is not easy to meet a genuine precious master, as fake masters are everywhere and masters with real attainment are very few. Being able to encounter a rare and precious master is definitely due to the accumulation of good karma in previous lives."
chapterbody 4: "It is not by chance that Grandmaster Lu appeared in this world. You are a rare and precious master replete with virtue and merit. The rare and precious masters that you yourself have met, such as Reverend Liaoming, His Eminence Sakya Zhengkong Rinpoche, His Holiness the 16th Karmapa, Vajra Acharya Thubten Dargye and so on, are all kind friends and companions on the path of cultivation from many previous lifetimes. These are not chance encounters or coincidences, but karmic affinity."
chapterbody 6: "When people who want to cultivate happen to meet you, and then take refuge in you, these are also not by chance or coincidence, but rather the thread of affinity has linked everyone together. It is a basic fact that you are Amitabha Buddha. It is also a fact that there are countless padmakumaras. All of your cultivation natures from previous lives have been awakened, bringing about the encounters between holy disciples and a rare and precious master. These are all facts."
chapterbody 8: I asked:
chapterbody 9: "What about the ones who leave?"
chapterbody 10: White Tara answered: "They are like the sun covered by clouds, like a sail boat blown off-course by a great wind, like a pearl necklace with a broken string, like eggs fallen from an overturned nest, like a wild goose separated from its flock... this human world is indeed not perfect. There will be a few camels amongst a flock of sheep, and insects can also grow in the rice barrel. At this time, a rare and precious master is also aware that this is Buddhadharma encountering baffling and inexplicable enmity, which is also a kind of teaching, best remedied by silence and patient endurance."
chapterbody 12: "Is there no way of saving them?"
chapterbody 13: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 14: "Bad karma and pollution from this world cause these disciples to leave their rare and precious master, this is also a fact. Their names are all listed in the . Only when their faith, intelligence and wisdom arise again, and through the teaching and empowerment of Re- Purification, can they then be rescued!"
chapterbody 16: "It is because of the fact of these desertions that you, Grandmaster Lu, thoroughly understand the impermanence of conditioned phenomena, the selflessness of Dharma, nirvana and quiescence. This enabled the transformation of your heart to renounce the human world, to distance yourself from worldly life, and thus become enlightened about the true nature of reality. Isn't it so?"
chapterbody 18: I asked:
chapterbody 19: "Why do disciples cast aside their genuine precious master?"
chapterbody 20: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 21: Their faith is not lasting, and if they are only momentarily touched by you, that is not enough.
chapterbody 22: Their faith is weak, while worldly inclinations are too strong.
chapterbody 23: They regard themselves too highly, and believe that their rare and precious master is ordinary.
chapterbody 24: They believe that they themselves are the leaders, and disregard their rare and precious master.
chapterbody 25: They deceive their rare and precious master, to gain profit and benefits.
chapterbody 26: Utterly disregarding their rare and precious master, they believe themselves to be more capable.
chapterbody 27: They obtain worldly gains under the guise of Buddhism.
chapterbody 28: They use their rare and precious master as a pretext, with the intention of burning the bridge after crossing the river.
chapterbody 29: Not actualizing cultivation, they have no proof of attainment.
chapterbody 30: Wandering about, having wavering faith, they follow profitable opportunities wherever these arise.
chapterbody 31: Exaggerating supernatural powers, they boast falsely about having attained enlightenment, proclaiming to be more powerful than the rare and precious master, mystifying fellow cultivators.
chapterbody 32: Having a fondness for stirring up troubles, they lack Buddhadharma in their hearts.
chapterbody 33: They are like wandering tricksters.
chapterbody 34: Their speech and actions are not in accord with Dharma, but they use their wits and twisted reasoning to justify and explain their inconsistencies.
chapterbody 35: Erroneous views.
chapterbody 36: I asked:
chapterbody 37: "Any more?"
chapterbody 38: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 39: "Are these not enough?"
chapterbody 51: Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms

chapterbody 2: "There are rare and precious masters; are there no fake ones?"
chapterbody 4: "How could there be none? Not only so, there are as many fake masters as there are carps in the river, while genuine precious masters are very difficult to find."
chapterbody 6: "Who are the fake masters?"
chapterbody 8: "All of their names have been listed in the !"
chapterbody 9: I asked:
chapterbody 10: "How could it be him or her?"
chapterbody 12: The ultimate aim of Buddhadharma is only for "mastering one's own life and death," "the extinction of afflictions," and "disseminating Buddhadharma so that others may become emancipated too."
chapterbody 13: When one abandons these three main goals, then one is a fake master without a doubt. For instance:
chapterbody 14: An erudite authority in Buddhism, who has studied and researched the Tripitaka in great depth and detail, and is able to understand all the Sutra, Vinaya, and Abhidharma Pitakas, but if he does not engage in actual cultivation, then this person is a fake master.
chapterbody 16: For instance:
chapterbody 17: An eminent Buddhist scholar who has read more than three hundred books about Vajrayana Buddhism and has published quite a bit on this topic, but if this person has never taken refuge, received empowerment, and engaged in actual practice, then this person is a fake master.
chapterbody 19: For instance:
chapterbody 20: There is a great master of a Buddhist sect, who likes politics and focuses wholeheartedly on political affairs, more so than his/her personal cultivation. Although this person may be renowned worldwide, he/she is similarly also a fake master.
chapterbody 22: For instance:
chapterbody 23: There is a Vajrayana practitioner who has taken refuge, received empowerment, and uses money and persuasiveness to procure the empowerment to be an acharya master. Thereafter, this person proceeds to deceive the world. Lying to everyone that he/she cultivates when in reality he/she does not. This is also a fake master.
chapterbody 25: For instance:
chapterbody 26: There is an acharya who frequently speaks about supernatural powers, exaggerating his/her own clairvoyance, speaking of clairvoyance as if it were the same as Buddhadharma, equating supernatural powers with attainment, and other such pretentious and inflated talk. Eventually, this becomes a habit.
chapterbody 27: In actual fact these are all bluff and bluster.
chapterbody 28: This kind of master is certainly a fake master.
chapterbody 30: For instance:
chapterbody 31: An acharya who accumulates money, more so than cultivating his/her heart to become one with Buddhadharma, then this is definitely a fake master.
chapterbody 33: For instance:
chapterbody 34: There is an acharya who pours his/her heart into building up his/her own position and fame, (wanting to climb the social ladder) by closely associating with world-famous people, and wants to become the most well-known master. (Overzealousness in climbing the social ladder.) In reality, his/her heart is not yet one with Buddhadharma; this is definitely a fake master.
chapterbody 36: For instance:
chapterbody 37: An acharya who acquires a lot of disciples, and exerts great effort in expanding his/her territory, while his/her heart is not yet one with Buddhadharma; this is certainly a fake master.
chapterbody 39: All in all:
chapterbody 40: An acharya, who focuses mostly on worldly gain, more so than transcendental attainment.
chapterbody 41: An acharya, who focuses on outward appearance, and overlooks inner cultivation.
chapterbody 42: An acharya, who is obsessed with clairvoyance, and does not know the ultimate fruition.
chapterbody 43: An acharya, who pursues fame and gain, false glory and flattery, and has not really actualized cultivation.
chapterbody 44: An acharya, who has committed many evil deeds, and does not abide by the precepts.
chapterbody 45: An acharya, who has turned away from his/her own Lineage Guru. All these, are fake masters.
chapterbody 51: Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms

chapterbody 2: "Are we, True Buddha School, walking on the regular path of cultivation?"
chapterbody 4: "There are some disciples of True Buddha School who are on the right path, however, most people are very corrupted."
chapterbody 6: White Tara said:
chapterbody 7: "Some people only pay lip service to cultivation and use True Buddha Dharma as a facade. They self-proclaim to be cultivators and dharma propagators, but what they love most are material and worldly things. There are far too many of these fake cultivators."
chapterbody 8: I asked:
chapterbody 9: "What is our fault?"
chapterbody 10: "A plateful of scattered sand."
chapterbody 12: "Many do not have the slightest bit of mastery in cultivation, and do not abide by Buddhist regulations, while fervently going after worldly pursuits. Disciples with attainment will not be many, and only a minority of people will be able to free themselves from the cycle of reincarnation."
chapterbody 13: I asked:
chapterbody 14: "What about masters?"
chapterbody 16: "Regarding themselves too highly; believing themselves to be the greatest leaders; are number one in social networking; are attached to erroneous views; do not have a transcendental mind of renunciation; covet money and material things; their speech and behavior deviate from lineage; not carrying out actual cultivation at all; speaking falsely about supernatural powers; beguiling believers; hypnotizing and brainwashing others; building up his/her influence with the intention of breaking away to establish his/her own independent sect..."
chapterbody 17: As I listened, my face turned pale.
chapterbody 18: I asked:
chapterbody 19: "What will happen to them?"
chapterbody 20: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 21: "Descent into the three lower realms."
chapterbody 22: I asked:
chapterbody 23: "How can they be saved?"
chapterbody 24: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 25: "Abiding by precepts is the foundation of Buddhadharma. If disciples do not abide by precepts, then True Buddha School would be a plateful of scattered sand."
chapterbody 26: "What is the greatest failing of masters?"
chapterbody 27: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 28: "Going further and further away from their Root Guru, and believing themselves to be right. Their so-called devotion and reverence are only on the surface. They are unable to receive lineage empowerment, and not only so, they are unable to benefit others, only benefitting themselves. The number of greedy masters is increasing, and this is extremely alarming and to be feared."
chapterbody 30: I asked:
chapterbody 31: "Which precepts are suitable for which people?"
chapterbody 32: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 33: The Five Precepts are suitable for people who have just received the refuge empowerment.
chapterbody 34: The Eight Precepts are suitable for lay cultivators who practice at home.
chapterbody 35: The Sramanera Precepts are suitable for people who wish to free themselves from the reincarnation cycle.
chapterbody 36: The Bodhisattva Precepts are suitable for cultivators who practice loving-kindness, compassion, joy, and equanimity.
chapterbody 37: The Bhikkhu Pratimoksha is suitable for monks.
chapterbody 38: The Bhikkhuni Pratimoksha is suitable for nuns.
chapterbody 39: The Aspiration Bodhicitta Vows are suitable for people who have a heart of equanimity, regarding themselves and others equally.
chapterbody 40: The Action Bodhicitta Vows are suitable for people who regard others more than themselves.
chapterbody 41: The Fifty Stanzas of Guru Devotion are suitable for Vajrayana cultivators.
chapterbody 42: The Fourteen Root Tantric Vows are suitable for yogi cultivators.
chapterbody 43: The Complete Monastic Code (Pratimoksha) are suitable for great cultivators.
chapterbody 44: I asked:
chapterbody 45: "Are these important?"
chapterbody 46: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 47: "Of course they are important. If the Sangha or household cultivators are completely without precepts, the whole True Buddha School would be too loose and lax. There would be such things as: cultivators of Mahayana without bodhicitta; yogis who do not abide by samaya; masters without actual cultivation; cultivators who are not zealous about attainment, but instead the obtaining of worldly items; cravings for fame, fortune, and sensual pleasures. In this way, success in cultivation would be very difficult, and instead it would be very easy to fall into the Hell of Uninterrupted Suffering."
chapterbody 48: I was silent.

chaptername: 041 The Danger of Falling into Traps
chapterbody: ultivators who have left their homes, namely bhikkhus and bhikkhunis, have single-mindedly renounced worldly life in pursuit of the holy spiritual path.
chapterbody 2: I asked White Tara:
chapterbody 3: "Do bhikkhus and bhikkhunis ever go astray?"
chapterbody 4: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 6: I asked White Tara to please explain and give examples.
chapterbody 8: For instance: A bhikkhuni, whose "fame" was so great, that people all around the world knew of her.
chapterbody 9: This thus created the danger of falling into a trap. For, in order to keep her fame, she had to focus all her attention on this matter, and this bhikkhuni became more worldly instead. Becoming worldly (when worldliness surpasses the transcendental) is a deviant path.
chapterbody 10: This bhikkhuni had forgotten about cultivation and enlightenment, and about renouncing worldliness.
chapterbody 11: For instance: An eminent master, who wished to receive the glory of winning the Nobel Prize.
chapterbody 12: Day and night, he would spend his time and energy on devising ways to meet the requirements to qualify as the Nobel Peace Prize is not easily acquired. If one keeps dwelling on attaining the Nobel Peace Prize, one would certainly fall into a trap, and not be able to cultivate to enlightenment and attain the "absolute."
chapterbody 14: For instance: A Zen master, who built the biggest Zen temple in the world.
chapterbody 15: Building Zen temples is not wrong. However, the building of one great temple after another requires a lot of money and energy.
chapterbody 16: The gain of money is inestimable.
chapterbody 17: The expense of mind and energy is incalculable.
chapterbody 18: If the Zen master only concentrates on the building of one great temple after another, then how will he be able to cultivate to enlightenment?
chapterbody 19: Forgetting the "renunciation of worldliness" is a trap, and is the danger.
chapterbody 21: For instance: Another Zen master, who aspired to establish a Buddhist college. This is also not a bad cause.
chapterbody 22: However, establishing a college requires money and resources, as well as manpower, as well as management and maintenance staff, as well as faculty members, as well as administrators, as well as land, as well as students...
chapterbody 23: An ordained Zen master pouring himself into the establishment of a college, while the college is also not completely dedicated to teaching Buddhadharma. Rather, the courses offered are more worldly than spiritual.
chapterbody 24: Is this appropriate?
chapterbody 25: Bhikkhus and bhikkhunis, please contemplate this carefully.
chapterbody 26: Is this a wayward path in cultivation?
chapterbody 28: For instance: Another well-known master who has a million disciples, and every disciple wishes to meet and talk to him.
chapterbody 29: If this master puts all his attention and energy on his disciples, wanting to acquire more disciples as well as wishing to meet with his disciples every day, then in this way, having many disciples has instead become this master's dangerous trap.
chapterbody 30: His cultivation towards enlightenment, renunciation to attain the "absolute" has developed an obstacle.
chapterbody 31: White Tara said:
chapterbody 32: Family and relatives can obstruct the holy path.
chapterbody 33: Engaging in management can obstruct the holy path.
chapterbody 34: Engaging in business can obstruct the holy path.
chapterbody 35: Even worse:
chapterbody 36: Fame can obstruct the holy path.
chapterbody 37: Glory and reputation can obstruct the holy path.
chapterbody 38: The worst of all:
chapterbody 39: Indulgence in the enjoyment of pleasures will obstruct the holy path.
chapterbody 40: Laziness and laxity will obstruct the holy path.
chapterbody 41: Indulgence in sleep will obstruct the holy path.…
chapterbody 42: If there are any obstacles hindering your cultivation towards enlightenment and the renunciation of worldly entanglements, these could all be dangerous traps, and it is then necessary to adjust one's footsteps!

chapterbody 2: "In the eyes of buddhas, how do they regard sentient beings?"
chapterbody 4: "Naive."
chapterbody 6: "How do bodhisattvas regard sentient beings?"
chapterbody 8: "Naive."
chapterbody 9: I asked again:
chapterbody 10: "How does White Tara regard sentient beings?"
chapterbody 12: "Still naive."
chapterbody 13: I said:
chapterbody 14: "Didn't you say that sentient beings are amazing? That sentient beings are not sentient beings and are sentient beings? That buddhas arise in the world because of sentient beings? Then how are sentient beings naive?"
chapterbody 16: "Sentient beings are indeed amazing. From their naiveté, they are able to see and validate buddha-nature. That's what's truly amazing."
chapterbody 17: I asked:
chapterbody 18: "What makes this possible?"
chapterbody 19: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 20: "The teachings of Tathagata!"
chapterbody 24: White Tara said:
chapterbody 25: "Although some people already know to cultivate Buddhadharma, however, they are still very naive. Grandmaster Lu, you must teach them!"
chapterbody 26: I asked:
chapterbody 27: "How do I teach them?"
chapterbody 28: White Tara said:
chapterbody 29: Fishing while trying to cultivate the Four Immeasurable Vows of loving-kindness, compassion, joy, and equanimity. This is very naive.
chapterbody 30: Asking the Root Guru for empowerment every day, but never keeping the samaya of purifying one's own body, speech, and mind. This is very naive.
chapterbody 31: Listening to Root Guru's dharma discourses every day, but unable to put into practice what was taught, and simply not able to fulfill the teachings. This is naive.
chapterbody 32: On one hand, wishing to do good deeds, but on the other hand, behaving wickedly; on one hand, practicing repentance, but on the other hand, repeating one's transgressions. Isn't this naive?
chapterbody 33: Following the Root Guru very closely, and attending to the Root Guru. However, still engaging in misbehavior and unwholesome actions. Isn't this naive?
chapterbody 34: Having already received the empowerment to be an acharya master, one is already equivalent to being a teacher. However, one is still engaged in secular business dealings, dedicating a large amount of energy and mind on worldly business. This is naive.
chapterbody 35: Already a master, but making supplications every day, and finding ways to fulfill these wishes. What are these wishes? To receive the Nobel Prize, Peace Prize, Goodwill Ambassador Award, Benevolence Award, Achievement Award, Lifetime Award, Good Deed Award, Light of the Society Award... Fostering worldly honor and prestige. Isn't this naive!
chapterbody 36: Already a master, but still criticizing this person as not good or saying that person is bad, and commenting about the rights and wrongs of others. This is indeed naive and pitiable.
chapterbody 37: Cultivators going forth to receive various precepts and vows, but are unable to abide by them. This is also naive.
chapterbody 38: One's habitual tendencies have not changed in the slightest bit, yet claiming to be cultivating the paramita of patient endurance, this is really naive.
chapterbody 39: Being fearful and apprehensive every day, yet claiming to be cultivating the paramita of meditative stability, this is really naive.
chapterbody 40: Always busily concerned with worldly affairs, leaving no time for Vajrayana practice, yet claiming to be cultivating the paramita of diligent effort, this is really naive.
chapterbody 41: Self-proclaiming to have attained enlightenment, but then saying that one has been aggrievedly insulted, mistreated, discriminated against, slandered... This type of person has not yet attained enlightenment. Isn't this naive?
chapterbody 42: Self-proclaiming to have become a buddha, yet not being willing to benefit sentient beings. Isn't this naive?
chapterbody 43: Self-proclaiming to be a bodhisattva, yet not having a compassionate heart. Isn't this naive?
chapterbody 44: Self-proclaiming to have become accomplished in cultivation, yet being jealous of others. Isn't this naive?
chapterbody 45: Having already taken refuge, and having received the empowerment to practice, as well as the sadhanas of the practices, and also having set up a cultivation shrine, but in the end, not having any time to cultivate dharma. Isn't this very naive?

chaptername: 043 Begin with the Practice of Antidotes
chapterbody 2: "Among all the methods of cultivation, where should one begin?"
chapterbody 4: "The practice of antidotes."
chapterbody 6: "Would it be necessary to permanently practice antidotes?"
chapterbody 8: "One has to practice antidotes until there is no longer any need for antidotes, and one understands and has realized one's own buddha-nature, and can spontaneously and freely move through the course of one's existence. At that time even when one does not practice any antidotes, it will also be an antidote; this is the highest state."
chapterbody 9: I said:
chapterbody 10: "I understand."
chapterbody 12: I then asked:
chapterbody 13: "What are the different antidotes?"
chapterbody 14: White Tara answered: The greatest antidotes are:
chapterbody 15: Using a transcendental mind of renunciation as an antidote to worldliness.
chapterbody 16: Using bodhicitta as an antidote to Theravada.
chapterbody 17: Using empty-nature as an antidote to reincarnation.
chapterbody 19: Common antidotes are:
chapterbody 20: Using precepts to remedy transgressions.
chapterbody 21: Using charity and generosity to remedy stinginess.
chapterbody 22: Using diligence to remedy lethargy.
chapterbody 23: Using patient endurance to remedy thoughts of anger.
chapterbody 24: Using meditation to remedy confusion and distractedness.
chapterbody 25: Using wisdom to remedy the Five Poisons.
chapterbody 27: Also:
chapterbody 28: Being mindful of death can remedy one's laxity.
chapterbody 29: Being mindful of the suffering of reincarnation can remedy one's unwholesome karma.
chapterbody 30: Thinking about the bliss of heaven can remedy one's attachment to this life.
chapterbody 31: Thinking of kind affinities can remedy all negative affinities.
chapterbody 32: Being mindful of karmic retribution can remedy even the smallest and most minor misbehavior.
chapterbody 33: Thinking of one's Root Guru can remedy the loss of Root Guru's empowerment.
chapterbody 34: Thinking of buddhas and one's personal deity can remedy the loss of the power of assimilation by one's personal deity.
chapterbody 35: Thinking of one's guardian deity can remedy the loss of protection from one's personal guardian deity.
chapterbody 37: Furthermore:
chapterbody 38: Pursuing greater peace and happiness for one's future lives can be an antidote to falling into the three lower realms.
chapterbody 39: Pursuing the diligent accumulation of benevolent deeds can be an antidote to reincarnation.
chapterbody 40: Pursuing liberation can be an antidote to transmigration through the six realms of existence.
chapterbody 41: (I would say this: what a cultivator seeks is not worldly fame, gain, sensual pleasures, and enjoyment, but rather, not falling into the three lower realms, and even more so, neither falling into the human nor heavenly realm, ultimately not entering into any of the six realms of transmigration, validating and attaining liberation on the path of enlightenment, thereafter becoming truly unhindered, abiding in spontaneous accomplishment.)
chapterbody 43: In addition:
chapterbody 44: Seek and follow a genuine precious master, as an antidote to masters with wrong views.
chapterbody 45: Seek and follow wholesome dharma companions, as an antidote to unwholesome friends.
chapterbody 46: Use the repentance sadhana to remedy one's faults.
chapterbody 47: Use Re-Purification to remedy broken precepts and vows.
chapterbody 48: Use pure faith to remedy doubt.
chapterbody 49: Use benefitting others to remedy envy.
chapterbody 50: Use equality to remedy jealousy.
chapterbody 51: Use simple living to remedy impurities.
chapterbody 52: Use the awareness of no difference between self and others to remedy prejudice and bias in saving others.
chapterbody 53: Practice the purification of the body to remedy impure actions.

chapterbody 2: "Do you know what mara is?"
chapterbody 4: "Mahesvara Mara, Mara of Afflictions, Mara of the Five Aggregates, and Mara of Death. These are the four maras that Hevajra tramples upon."
chapterbody 6: "Yes, these are the four maras. But, do you know? The greatest mara is the mara of attachment to self. Mara is not external at all, but is within oneself. One needs to slay the mara from within, then external maras would become ineffectual."
chapterbody 8: "Do you understand what is the mara from within?"
chapterbody 9: I answered:
chapterbody 10: "Attachment to self, and attachment to phenomena."
chapterbody 12: "Do you know how to get rid of it?"
chapterbody 13: I answered:
chapterbody 14: "With the sword of prajna!"
chapterbody 16: "What you said is correct. But it's a pity that in this world, who has the sword of prajna? Among great and renowned masters, great dharma masters, great Zen masters, eminent monks, and eminent living buddhas, who has the sword of prajna?"
chapterbody 17: I asked:
chapterbody 18: "If all these renowned cultivators don't have the sword of prajna, then who has the sword of prajna?"
chapterbody 19: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 20: "Very, very few people. Grandmaster Lu is one of the very, very few."
chapterbody 24: "Look!" White Tara said:
chapterbody 25: People with great authority and influence are bound by the mara of arrogance, and are unable to become cultivators.
chapterbody 26: People with great wealth are trapped by the mara of money, and are unable to become cultivators.
chapterbody 27: People in eminent positions are tightly bound to their position. How would it be possible for them to humble themselves for cultivation?
chapterbody 28: People in general have their eyes blinded by the mara of ignorance, to the extent that they are not able to become cultivators.
chapterbody 29: Entrepreneurs are restricted by the mara of riches and busyness. It would be very difficult for them to become cultivators.
chapterbody 30: Distinguished scholars are obstructed by the mara of knowledge. It would be very difficult for them to become cultivators.
chapterbody 31: Distinguished scientists are limited by the mara of science. It would be very difficult for them to become cultivators.
chapterbody 32: Eminent cultivators are trapped by their own respective domains. It would be very difficult for them to escape the grasp of the mara of domains.
chapterbody 33: Eminent cultivators are bound by the mara of sectarianism. It would be very difficult for them to become genuinely liberated cultivators.
chapterbody 34: Eminent cultivators bound by the mara of big temples will always be busy with matters of the temple.
chapterbody 35: Eminent cultivators trapped by their multitude of disciples would be unable to break away from these fetters and become free.
chapterbody 36: Eminent cultivators trapped by the mara of worldly matters would be unable to break free to attain enlightenment and truly realize buddha nature.
chapterbody 37: Some people are bound by their children and family
chapterbody 38: Some people are bound by lovely women
chapterbody 39: Some people are bound by people they detest
chapterbody 40: Some people are bound by name brand goods and accessories
chapterbody 41: Some people are bound by beauty
chapterbody 42: Some people are bound by sweet talk
chapterbody 43: Some people are bound by what they crave or love
chapterbody 44: Some people are bound by pets
chapterbody 45: Some people are bound by their habitual tendencies
chapterbody 46: White Tara told me:
chapterbody 47: "Attachment to great bliss will cause one to become bound by the mara of great bliss. Attachment to clear light will cause one to become bound by the mara of clear light. Attachment to emptiness will cause one to become bound by the mara of emptiness. Even attachment to merit, attachment to becoming buddha... All are bondages!"
chapterbody 48: I asked:
chapterbody 49: "So, what constitutes a sword of prajna?"
chapterbody 50: White Tara answered:

chapterbody 2: "In this world, there is more pain and sorrow than happiness. On the surface, it seems that sadness and happiness are opposites. In actual fact, sadness and happiness arise from the same mind."
chapterbody 4: "True. Love and hatred also arise from the same mind."
chapterbody 6: "That's why all phenomena arise from the one mind."
chapterbody 7: I asked: "Is the human realm full of hardship and suffering?"
chapterbody 8: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 9: "Yes."
chapterbody 10: White Tara said:
chapterbody 11: "In the human world there is only one type of person, who has no suffering or happiness, who feels neither suffering nor happiness. This kind of people have transcended beyond the ordinary and entered the holy stream; their bodies are in this world, but their minds are not."
chapterbody 12: I asked: "If their minds are not in the human realm, then where are their minds residing?"
chapterbody 13: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 14: "Taoist of No-Mind! Don't you understand?"
chapterbody 15: I understood, and nodded my head.
chapterbody 19: White Tara said:
chapterbody 20: "Do not think that people in pursuit of the Five Poisons, people who pursue wealth, sensual pleasures, fame, food, and sleep, and people who are greedy, angry, ignorant, arrogant, and jealous would attain happiness. In reality, hiding deep beneath this sort of happiness is also just a kind of sorrow and suffering!"
chapterbody 21: I asked: "How so?"
chapterbody 22: White Tara replied:
chapterbody 23: People who are greedy have a great amount of anxiety, and their minds are often in a state of anguish, afraid of being found out. People whose minds have a great amount of enmity, when they lash out in anger, their bodies are already submerged in the burning sea of suffering. People who are ignorant are like birds and beasts, the strong preying on the weak. Their minds are confused, and they are unable to extricate themselves, alternately floating and sinking. Isn't this painful?
chapterbody 24: People who are arrogant are already suffering from the disdain of others. People who are greatly jealous tend to be ambitious and scheming. In fact, envy itself is a kind of pain.
chapterbody 26: White Tara continued:
chapterbody 27: Grandmaster Lu, take a look: People who cannot adjust their minds, are all suffering! People who cannot get rid of their attachment to themselves, are all suffering! People who are never content and never fulfilled, are all suffering! People who cannot get rid of material desires, are all suffering! People who cannot endure humiliation, are all suffering!
chapterbody 28: Those who are envious are suffering!
chapterbody 29: Those who are jealous are suffering!
chapterbody 30: Those who frame others are suffering!
chapterbody 31: Those who are indolent are suffering!
chapterbody 32: Those who torment others and those being tormented, are all suffering! To gain is also suffering! To lose is also suffering!
chapterbody 33: The worries of gaining and losing are all suffering!
chapterbody 34: White Tara added:
chapterbody 35: Beings in the wheel of reincarnation, are all suffering! People in pursuit of special relationships, are all suffering! People in the highest positions, are all suffering! People in the midst of beauty and sensual pleasures, are all suffering the most! People in the midst of obsessions or infatuations, are all suffering!
chapterbody 36: Those who yearn are those who are suffering.
chapterbody 37: I asked:
chapterbody 38: "How does one not suffer?"
chapterbody 39: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 40: "Understand one's own mind!"

chapterbody 2: "What is the most crucial key to success in cultivating Vajrayana Buddhism?"
chapterbody 4: "One's Root Guru."
chapterbody 6: "Why?"
chapterbody 8: "Vajrayana cultivation begins from receiving the refuge empowerment, and this refuge empowerment is given by one's Root Guru. All Buddhadharma is transmitted by one's Root Guru. Without the Root Guru's teaching of pivotal keys and pith instructions, how would all cultivators be able to know these? Even if one has the cultivation procedure, but does not have the power of empowerment and blessing from the lineage dharma stream, one will also not be able to attain accomplishment. The cultivator would also not know the Root Guru's pivotal keys and pith instructions leading to accomplishment, hence his/her cultivation would be futile. In short, in Vajrayana Buddhism, the Root Guru is the embodiment of the Three Jewels: Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha. Hence, one's Lineage Root Guru is the most crucial key to accomplishment in the cultivation of Buddhadharma."
chapterbody 9: I asked:
chapterbody 10: "If one does not respect one's guru, would he/she also be able to attain accomplishment?"
chapterbody 12: "That would be a joke. If one does not respect one's Root Guru, then one would not respect Dharma. If one does not respect Dharma, then one would not practice sincerely and diligently. These are all interrelated."
chapterbody 13: I asked:
chapterbody 14: "What if one only respects the Dharma, or only diligently practices cultivation?"
chapterbody 16: "If one is without the empowerment and blessing from one's Root Guru, then it would be like having firewood but no kindling material, forever unable to be lit. How can the torch of dharma cultivation be lit without any tinder?"
chapterbody 17: White Tara said:
chapterbody 18: Look! Cultivators who have broken their samaya, are like this: They only proclaim their attainment in Vajrayana Buddhism, but would not mention the empowerment they received from their Root Guru, concealing the name of their Root Guru, either afraid of mentioning it, or intentionally obscuring it.
chapterbody 19: They only proclaim that all Buddhadharma comes from the heavens, or was taught to them in their dreams, or taught personally to them by the buddhas and bodhisattvas, or transmitted to them by mysterious sages. They have no lineage, no Root Guru. They may say that they received instructions from high spiritual entities supporting them. Those who proclaim thus have utterly forsaken Vajrayana Buddhism, and are not in keeping with Vajrayana Buddhism.
chapterbody 20: (White Tara pointed out: These so-called instructions from the heavens, dreams, buddhas, bodhisattvas, mysterious sages, or high spiritual entities could all be manifestations of mara. If one person is led astray by mara, then this person's followers or disciples would all be led astray by mara.)
chapterbody 21: They only proclaim their own names, exaggerating and aggrandizing their reputation, casting their Root Guru to the recesses of their minds. Or they proclaim their own accomplishments to have long surpassed the accomplishments of their Root Guru, and that their Root Guru is insignificant!
chapterbody 22: They only proclaim that they are pure, and defame their Root Guru, blaming their Root Guru for all failings and mistakes.
chapterbody 23: They only proclaim the greatness of their own accomplishments, while deriding their Root Guru to have long become beneath them, that their Root Guru's level is inferior to their own, judging and casting aspersions on their Root Guru's level of attainment.
chapterbody 24: (In fact, disciples should not speculate on their Root Guru's level of accomplishment.)
chapterbody 25: They regard the group of people that they are leading as belonging to themselves, wanting to pull them away from their Root Guru, and with the intention to rival their Root Guru in the future.
chapterbody 26: They only pretend to respect their Root Guru, and in reality, their heart is already set on betrayal. They secretly pirate their Root Guru's sadhanas, and teach them as their own. They embezzle the money meant as offerings for their Root Guru. They steal the material items meant as offerings for their Root Guru.
chapterbody 27: They lie to their Root Guru.
chapterbody 28: They proclaim: That their Root Guru is not important. That they have reached enlightenment, although they have not yet received validation or approval from their Root Guru. That their Root Guru has no empowering ability, and claim that they are the ones who actually have the ability to give empowerment.…
chapterbody 29: I asked:
chapterbody 30: "Why are there disciples like these?"
chapterbody 31: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 32: "This is the human world."

chaptername: 047 The Secret to Success in Cultivation
chapterbody 2: "What is the secret to success in cultivation?"
chapterbody 4: "It all starts with taking refuge in a Root Guru replete with virtue and merit."
chapterbody 6: "What is a Root Guru replete with virtue and merit?"
chapterbody 8: "A full explanation would be too lengthy, nonetheless the simplest answer would be that this master has truly become accomplished in cultivation, and has the keys to success in cultivation."
chapterbody 9: I asked:
chapterbody 10: "Is it important to have supernatural ability?"
chapterbody 12: "Only when one has purity of body, speech, and mind will clairvoyance naturally develop. Otherwise, it would be due to ghosts or evil spirits. Clairvoyance is not important, and does not represent accomplishment in cultivation."
chapterbody 13: I asked:
chapterbody 14: "I have an opinion, having purity of body, speech, and mind is of course success in cultivation. But doesn't the clairvoyance which arises also indicate successful cultivation?"
chapterbody 16: "Supernatural power is often confused with accomplishment in cultivation. In truth, clairvoyance is only the side effect or byproduct of successful cultivation. Moreover, amongst cultivators in this present age, there are not many with true clairvoyance. Most people are spirit mediums rather than having true clairvoyant powers. Their ability to know the karma linking past, present, and future lives, is also from spirit channeling. Being able to enter and exit the four dhyana states of meditation, also does not count as accomplishment in cultivation."
chapterbody 18: I asked:
chapterbody 19: "What are the keys to success in cultivation?"
chapterbody 20: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 21: Attending to one's Root Guru is very important, and receiving pivotal keys and pith instructions is very important.
chapterbody 22: Sincerely respect your Root Guru without affectations, and do not let your Root Guru worry.
chapterbody 23: Stop all bad behaviors.
chapterbody 24: Visualize your Root Guru as a crown ornament, and always carry your Root Guru above your head. Think of your Root Guru all the time, and in this way, there will always be empowerment.
chapterbody 25: Do not have even the slightest thought of going against the will and instructions of a Root Guru with virtue and merit. (I wonder, who would be able to fulfill this point?)
chapterbody 26: Diligently practice cultivation daily. Once a day, or three or four times a day. The important points are:
chapterbody 27: Visualization
chapterbody 28: Mantra recitation
chapterbody 29: Entering samadhi (entering of the deity into self and entering of self into the deity)
chapterbody 30: The important points of visualization are:
chapterbody 31: Facing one
chapterbody 32: Sitting on top of one's head
chapterbody 33: Becoming one
chapterbody 34: With regard to important keys and pith instructions, etch them clearly in your mind, and never disregard or neglect the absoluteness of these pivotal keys.
chapterbody 35: Bodhicitta is very important, a mind of renouncing worldliness is very important, Right Views are very important.
chapterbody 36: Abide resolutely by one's vows and precepts, and do not break any of the samaya vows. If broken, cultivation will not be successful.
chapterbody 37: Understanding the mind is very important. Understanding the mind is realizing enlightenment and seeing Tao. In this human world, there are not many people who are enlightened. You, Grandmaster Lu, are one who has truly attained enlightenment.
chapterbody 38: Enlightenment cannot be disclosed casually. Only to people who have reached a certain level of insight, can pointers be given to aid in revelation.
chapterbody 39: Cultivation to see buddha-nature is indeed important, and only seeing and validating buddha-nature can be counted as success in cultivation, while all the rest are not.
chapterbody 40: Reverently pay homage to your virtuous Root Guru.
chapterbody 41: Reverently pay homage to your Personal Deity.
chapterbody 42: Reverently pay homage to your Dharma Protector.
chapterbody 43: I asked:
chapterbody 44: "Who has succeeded in cultivation?"
chapterbody 45: White Tara answered: "Grandmaster Lu, you know it yourself!"

chaptername: 048 Dharma Teachings Pertaining to the Great Perfection
chapterbody 2: "The Nyingma School of Tibetan Tantric Buddhism cultivates Dzogchen, the Great Perfection; does White Tara have any special keys or pith instructions regarding this dharma?"
chapterbody 4: "I do have pith instructions, however, this is a great dharma practice of the highest level of cultivation, and it is very difficult for practitioners to reach this stage. It would be better for ordinary cultivators to take one step at a time and practice in a down-to-earth and sensible manner."
chapterbody 6: "Cultivation begins from the path of merit accumulation, then the path of preparation, until the path of seeing the way, the path of cultivating dharma, and finally arriving at the stage of completion. Which part of the path would the accomplishment of the Great Perfection belong to?"
chapterbody 8: "The accomplishment of the Great Perfection is the ultimate stage of completion."
chapterbody 9: I asked:
chapterbody 10: "The most subtle and refined awakening into Buddhahood?"
chapterbody 12: "Yes."
chapterbody 13: I asked:
chapterbody 14: "How is my cultivation of the Great Perfection?"
chapterbody 16: "You became Living Buddha Lian-sheng!"
chapterbody 18: I said:
chapterbody 19: "Would the White Tara please talk about the keys and pith instructions pertaining to the Great Perfection?"
chapterbody 20: White Tara said:
chapterbody 21: Please listen carefully. One element and not two - this is the important and pivotal key. Emptiness is form, form is emptiness. Reincarnation is nirvana, and affliction is bodhi.
chapterbody 22: Buddha and mara are one suchness. Clean and filthy are one suchness. Good and evil are one suchness.
chapterbody 23: So on and so forth...
chapterbody 25: I said:
chapterbody 26: "These concepts are incomprehensible to ordinary people."
chapterbody 27: White Tara said:
chapterbody 28: These can only be understood when a cultivator's "insight" has reached its peak; general cultivators would not understand.
chapterbody 29: The pith instructions for cultivation are:
chapterbody 30: - Being in a state of meditation, yet not conscious of meditating.
chapterbody 31: - Having no perception like the empty space.
chapterbody 32: - Having worries and afflictions is also equivalent to not having worries and afflictions.
chapterbody 33: - Having no attachment to the existence or end of True Buddha School. The existence of True Buddha School is also equivalent to the non-existence of True Buddha School; let alone others...
chapterbody 34: - You, I, and they do not exist.
chapterbody 35: - All forms are empty. (Wealth, sensual pleasures, reputation, food, and sleep)
chapterbody 36: - Confusion and fear simply do not exist. Confusion and fear do not arise.
chapterbody 37: - Reputation does not exist.
chapterbody 38: - Family and relatives do not exist.
chapterbody 39: - There is no love or hate.
chapterbody 40: - There is no sorrow, and there is no happiness.
chapterbody 41: - Understand the illusory nature of the material world. Form, sound, smell, taste, touch, and phenomena are illusions.
chapterbody 42: Even I do not exist.
chapterbody 43: I said:
chapterbody 44: "Isn't this all emptiness?"
chapterbody 45: White Tara said:
chapterbody 46: "This is dwelling in the vast emptiness."
chapterbody 47: I said:
chapterbody 48: "How can there be happiness in emptiness?"
chapterbody 49: White Tara said:
chapterbody 50: "To let go is happiness, enlightenment is happiness, being unhindered is happiness, abiding in peace is happiness, being carefree is happiness, no-self is happiness, a quiescent mind is happiness... Only these kinds of happiness are eternal great happiness."
chapterbody 53: "A pity! It is very difficult for people of this world to understand, and they also do not know."

chapterbody 2: "White Tara Bodhisattva, this is the last article of this book. Do you still have any other advice for us?"
chapterbody 4: "A first warning, a second warning, a third warning."
chapterbody 6: "Saying something once is enough, is there a need to repeat it again?"
chapterbody 8: "People are forgetful, or will knowingly disregard and violate the precepts and vows, or are unable to withstand temptations, or even believe that they will be able to get away or be spared. People forget that karma and retribution is universal and absolute. Those who have fallen and are in danger of rebirth in the three lower realms, need to cultivate the repentance and re-purification sadhanas, and cannot afford to be even the slightest bit negligent."
chapterbody 9: I asked:
chapterbody 10: "What else should dharma propagators take caution of?"
chapterbody 12: "When one often does not practice dharma, yet teaches others to practice regularly.
chapterbody 13: When one does not engage in actual cultivation, yet gives the refuge empowerment to others.
chapterbody 14: When one is not yet accomplished in yogic union, yet gives the empowerment for yogic union practices to others.
chapterbody 15: When one is not yet enlightened, but self-proclaims to be enlightened, and goes forth to certify the enlightenment of others.
chapterbody 16: When one is selfish and thinks only of one's own benefit, yet wants others to give generously.
chapterbody 17: When one's own conduct is not right, yet criticizes others' misbehavior.
chapterbody 18: When one does not have meditative stability, yet teaches others to meditate."
chapterbody 20: In short: Dharma propagators have to be examples for other cultivators.
chapterbody 21: If they are pretentious
chapterbody 22: Scheming
chapterbody 23: Hypocritical
chapterbody 24: Untruthful
chapterbody 25: Deceitful
chapterbody 26: Their names will all be listed in the .
chapterbody 27: I asked:
chapterbody 28: "Will abiding by the samaya vows and precepts cause Buddhadharma to become inflexible and dull, as well as impractical? Wouldn't this be not such a good thing?"
chapterbody 29: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 30: "Abide by the precepts, but not be bound by the precepts. When there is more time, we can talk about the many skillful and convenient means within this. The precepts can be applied in a flexible manner and are definitely not rigid."
chapterbody 31: I asked:
chapterbody 32: "What should cultivators take note of?"
chapterbody 33: White Tara answered:
chapterbody 34: "Not keeping the precepts, being lax in cultivation, believing oneself to have reached a high level in cultivation, disregarding one's own Root Guru. Such cultivators will certainly not succeed in cultivation."
chapterbody 36: White Tara continued:
chapterbody 37: "Those who like worldly things, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 38: Those who practice dharma erroneously, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 39: Those who deceive their guru, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 40: Those who are tricksters, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 41: Those who sell Buddhadharma, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 42: Those who live a corrupt life, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 43: Those who do not keep their samaya precepts, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 44: Those who only emphasize supernatural powers, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 45: Those who crave material things, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 46: Those who are ambitious with special agendas, will not attain accomplishment.
chapterbody 47: Those who are unable to endure patiently, will not attain accomplishment."
chapterbody 48: White Tara said:
chapterbody 49: "These are all people who lack Right Views. Even if they do not reincarnate into the three lower realms, they would also reincarnate as people with evil views, ogres, yakshas, rakshasas, or spirits."
chapterbody 50: I asked White Tara for further advice and teachings.
chapterbody 51: Highly Confidential Register of Those Fallen into the Three Lower Realms
chapterbody 53: "Please read this book carefully, engrave it upon your heart, and contemplate it time and time again."
